addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - API usage, tool use, Anthropic SDK usage. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

aiTitle: Add --port argument for serial port selection

content.text:
`--port` is already implemented in the CLI. It's wired up end to end:

- **Parsing** (`src/main.rs:476`): `"--port" => port = args.next()...`
- **Default** (`src/main.rs:463`): `/dev/ttyUSB0`
- **Stored in `Config.port`** (`src/main.rs:279`) and used to open the port (`src/main.rs:1231`): `serialport::new(&config.port, config.baud)`
- **Help text** (`src/main.rs:415-417`): documented under Global options
- **Test** (`src/main.rs:1472`): `--port /dev/ttyACM0` is asserted to land in `config.port`

So you can already run, e.g.:

```
rdwr_vn100 --port /dev/ttyACM0 get-hz
```

Want me to do anything beyond what's there — for example, change the default port, add a short `-p` alias, or auto-detect/list available serial ports when `--port` is omitted? Otherwise there's nothing to add.

message.content: whoops, I missed it, it's listed in globals!

content.text: No worries — it's right there under Global options. You're all set: `rdwr_vn100 --port /dev/ttyACM0 <command>`.

# Notes

This directory contains various notes and documentation related to the project.
Each file is organized by topic for easy reference.

By default there are chores/chores-*.md and todo.md. Chores are general notes
about tasks and todo.md contains short term tasks and their status.
The chores-NN files are numbered in sequence; as a chores file grows
large the next one is opened ([chores/chores-01.md](chores/chores-01.md)
is the active one in this template), and earlier ones are closed and
kept for history.


Examples chore file:
```
# Chores-01.md
 
General maintenance tasks and considerations for the project see other files for
more specific topics. A chore in a chores file provides quick information on the
how and why of a particular chore.

## Create a binary that lists jj info 

This binary should list the changeID, commitID, and description title
and using `jj-lib`
```

## Topic docs


## Workflow and conventions

Bot-facing workflow and conventions live in
[`../AGENTS.md`](../AGENTS.md):

- [Notes file conventions](../AGENTS.md#notes-file-conventions)
  — Todo format, Reference numbering, Notes references
  (`[[N]]` citation style), Markdown anchor links, Retiring
  Done entries.
- [Chores conventions](../AGENTS.md#chores-conventions) —
  section headers / Done entries exact-title rule, content
  rules, `Commits:` line format.
- [Code Conventions](../AGENTS.md#code-conventions) — doc
  comments, `// OK: …` on `unwrap*` calls, ask-on-ambiguity,
  stuck detection.

Per-cycle workflow lives in
[`cycle-protocol.md`](cycle-protocol.md):

- [Cycles](cycle-protocol.md#cycles) — three-phase shape
  (Preparation → Work → Close-out), `X.Y.Z-N` numbering,
  sub-cycles.
- [Per-commit flow](cycle-protocol.md#per-commit-flow) —
  cargo cycle (`fmt` / `clippy` / `test` / `install`),
  work + commit description review gates.
- [Commit description](cycle-protocol.md#commit-description)
  — Conventional Commits + `(version)` suffix; body shape
  per app vs `.claude` repo.
- [Pushing](cycle-protocol.md#pushing) — push policy,
  close-out shape, `.claude` cadence.

1	# AGENTS.md - Bot Instructions
2	
3	## Project Structure
4	
5	This project uses **two separate jj-git repos**:
6	
7	1. **App repo** (`/` — project root): Contains the application source code.
8	2. **Bot session repo** (`/.claude/`): Contains Claude Code session data.
9	
10	Both repos are managed with `jj` (Jujutsu), which coexists with git.
11	
12	**Committing vs pushing.** Default to `vc-x1 push` — it commits and
13	publishes both repos together, each carrying one `ochid:` pointing at
14	the other (see [Cycle Protocol](#cycle-protocol) and
15	[ochid trailers](#cross-repo-linking-ochid-trailers)). Use a bare
16	`jj commit` (see [jj Basics](#jj-basics)) only when:
17	
18	- the change will never be published — no `ochid:`;
19	- the commit will be squashed away before its series is pushed
20	  (loop-and-squash) — no `ochid:`; it disappears in the squash;
21	- the commit will be pushed later as a non-top commit — `jj commit`
22	  and add its `ochid:` now, because `vc-x1 push` stamps only the
23	  topmost commit it pushes, never the ancestors.
24	
25	## Repo Paths (relative from project root)
26	
27	- App repo: `.` (project root)
28	- Bot session repo: `.claude`
29	  (symlink from `~/.claude/projects/<path-to-project-root>/.claude`)
30	
31	## Working Directory
32	
33	Prefer staying in the project root. Use `-R` flags or absolute paths
34	to target other directories rather than `cd`. If `cd` seems necessary,
35	discuss with the user first — losing track of cwd causes subtle
36	command failures downstream.
37	
38	When invoking shell commands, prefer the shortest unambiguous path —
39	usually relative to cwd (`ls notes/`, not
40	`ls /home/wink/data/prgs/rust/vc-x1/notes/`). Long absolute paths
41	clutter the transcript without adding information. Out-of-workspace
42	paths (`/tmp/...`, `~/.config/...`) stay absolute. Read/Edit/Write
43	tool args also stay absolute — that's a tool-boundary constraint,
44	not a stylistic choice.
45	
46	Run **one command per shell invocation** — don't bundle several
47	steps into a single compound script (`a && b; c`). Bundling hides
48	which step produced which output and makes a failure ambiguous;
49	executing one at a time keeps the details visible and reviewable.
50	Exceptions are a genuine pipeline (`grep | sort`) or a tight,
51	inseparable pair where the join is the point.
52	
53	## File reads — read the slice you need
54	
55	Long notes files are appended to over time. Read only the
56	slice your task needs; grep or read further on demand.
57	
58	- **`notes/todo.md`** (the routine acquaint read) — first
59	  ~60 lines covers intro + `## In Progress` + the top of the
60	  ranked `## Todo` (priorities, #1 highest). `Read` with
61	  `offset=0, limit=60`. `## Ideas` sits below `## Todo`; read
62	  further only when chasing a lower-ranked entry, an Idea, a
63	  `[N]` ref, or auditing the whole list.
64	- **`notes/todo-backlog.md`** — the long-tail backlog
65	  (lower-priority entries below the ranked `## Todo`). Read
66	  only when picking up a backlog item; grep to locate it
67	  first.
68	- **`notes/bugs.md`** — the bug list. Small; read whole
69	  when triaging a bug or chasing the `## Bugs` pointer in
70	  todo.md.
71	- **`notes/done.md`** + **`notes/chores/chores-NN.md`** —
72	  historical / append-mostly. Scan headings first
73	  (`grep '^## ' notes/chores/chores-NN.md`), then read only
74	  the section you need.
75	
76	**Why:** the routine read should stay small. `notes/todo.md`
77	grows every cycle, so the backlog and bugs live in sibling
78	files rather than inline; the same "slice you need" rule
79	applies to historical files.
80	
81	## Memory
82	
83	Do not use the bot's per-project memory directory
84	(`~/.claude/projects/<path>/memory/`). In a dual-repo setup with
85	AGENTS.md it provides no capability AGENTS.md doesn't already cover,
86	and it loses on discoverability:
87	
88	- **AGENTS.md** — at the repo root, a well-known location for bot
89	  instructions, committed, reviewable, visible to every collaborator
90	  (human or bot).
91	- **Memory directory** — hidden under the user's home, tied to one
92	  machine, invisible to anyone but the bot, never diffed or reviewed.
93	
94	Easy for everyone to find beats convenient for the bot alone. Put
95	durable context in AGENTS.md (or committed `notes/`) instead.
96	
97	## Speculation marker
98	
99	Durable text the bot writes — AGENTS.md, `notes/`, commit bodies,
100	chores sections — should stick to observations and direct descriptions
101	of the code or data. If a mechanism, hypothesis, or causal claim
102	enters the text, prefix it with "We think ..." (a royal "we") so a
103	reader can tell the measured from the inferred.
104	
105	**Why:** unmarked speculation reads like evidence, and a future reader
106	(or the bot on a later session) can pick it up as a known fact when
107	it's not. Measured / inferred is a distinction worth keeping visible
108	in the written record.
109	
110	**How to apply:** observations and factual descriptions need no
111	marker. Prefix with "We think ..." (or a close variant like
112	"Our guess is ...") when the claim is a mechanism ("X wins
113	because Y caches better"), a cause ("the drift was due to thermal
114	state"), a prediction ("this should scale linearly"), or any
115	reasoning not directly supported by the data on hand.
116	
117	## jj Basics
118	
119	**Use jj, not git, for version-control operations** (status, log,
120	diff, commit, push, history rewrite). jj coexists with the git
121	backend, so the repo *can* be driven with raw `git`, but this
122	project's workflow — bookmarks, the working-copy `@` model, ochid
123	trailers — is expressed in jj terms; reaching for `git` invites
124	state that doesn't match the jj documentation here. There is no
125	`jj mv`: to move/rename a tracked file, just `mv` it on disk and
126	jj detects the rename by content.
127	
128	- `jj st -R .` / `jj st -R .claude` — show working copy status
129	- `jj log -R .` / `jj log -R .claude` — show commit log
130	- `jj commit -m "title" -m "body" -R <repo>` — finalize working copy into a commit
131	- `jj describe -m "title" -m "body" -R <repo>` — set description without committing
132	- `jj git push --bookmark <name> -R <repo>` — push a bookmark (no
133	  `--allow-new` flag; jj pushes new bookmarks without special flags)
134	- In jj, the working copy (@) is always a mutable commit being edited.
135	  `jj commit` finalizes it and creates a new empty working copy on top.
136	- The `.claude` repo always has uncommitted changes during an active
137	  session because session data updates continuously.
138	- `jj rebase` uses `--onto`/`-o` to name the destination(s).
139	
140	## Cross-repo linking (ochid trailers)
141	
142	The cross-reference between the app repo and the `.claude` bot
143	repo is what makes the dual-repo work: every commit points at its
144	counterpart in the other repo, so the "what" (code) and the
145	"why / how" (session) stay linked across time. That pointer is the
146	**ochid** (Other Change ID) git trailer.
147	
148	A **chid** is jj's change ID — a permanent identifier that
149	survives rebases and `describe`s (unlike the commit ID / git SHA,
150	which changes on rewrite). An **ochid** trailer carries the
151	counterpart commit's chid as a workspace-root-relative path:
152	
153	- Paths start with `/` — the workspace root, i.e. the app repo
154	  (vc-template-x1). `/.claude` is the bot session sub-repo.
155	- `ochid: /<chid>` references a change in the **app repo**.
156	- `ochid: /.claude/<chid>` references a change in the **`.claude`
157	  repo**.
158	
159	Trailers are blank-line-separated `key: value` lines at the end of
160	the commit body, using the chid's **12-character** prefix:
161	
162	```
163	ochid: /.claude/xvzvruqowktp   # points to a .claude repo change
164	ochid: /wtpmottvxqzl           # points to an app repo change
165	```
166	
167	How many, and which direction:
168	
169	- **Code-side commits** each carry one
170	  `ochid: /.claude/<.claude-chid>` — the `.claude` change ID.
171	- **The `.claude` commit** carries one `ochid: /<code-chid>` per
172	  code commit in that push. More than one occurs on a Merge
173	  non-ff close-out (one ochid per Work commit in the cycle).
174	
175	Use `vc-x1 chid -s code,bot -L` to capture the change IDs (first
176	line app, second `.claude`).
177	
178	### Resolvability
179	
180	A change ID travels with its commit: a **pushed** commit resolves
181	to the same chid in every clone — cloning the `.claude` repo gave
182	the published `main` tip the same chid as an existing clone. We
183	think jj carries the change ID in the git commit object, so it
184	survives `jj git clone` / fetch.
185	
186	The local-only case is the **working-copy `@`**: jj mints a fresh
187	random chid for `@` in each clone, so an unpushed `@` is never a
188	stable ochid target. This is why a `.claude` ochid names `@-`
189	(the last committed change), not `@`.
190	
191	### .vc-config.toml
192	
193	Each repo contains a `.vc-config.toml` that identifies its
194	location within the workspace, so tools resolve these paths
195	without repeating the workspace path in every trailer:
196	
197	```toml
198	# In vc-template-x1 (workspace root):
199	[workspace]
200	path = "/"
201	
202	# In .claude (sub-repo):
203	[workspace]
204	path = "/.claude"
205	```
206	
207	## Prose form
208	
209	Long-lived prose on this project follows one basic shape: a short intro
210	that explains the *why* or the high-level *what*, then a `-` bullet
211	list for the details. Wrap lines at ≤72 cols (bullet continuations
212	indent two spaces). Avoid wall-of-prose paragraphs — they hide the
213	structure that bullets make scannable.
214	
215	Surfaces that use this shape:
216	
217	- Module / function / struct / field doc comments in `.rs` files —
218	  see [Doc comments](#doc-comments-on-every-file-function-and-method).
219	- Commit message bodies (both app-repo and session-repo). The
220	  ≤50-col title is the commit-specific add-on; see
221	  [Per-commit flow](notes/cycle-protocol.md#per-commit-flow).
222	- Chore descriptions in `notes/chores/chores-NN.md` — see
223	  [Chores section content](#chores-section-content--no-edit-list-git-is-the-record).
224	- Todo and Done entries in `notes/todo.md` when an entry needs more
225	  than one line of detail. Pure one-liners are still fine.
226	
227	Bullet *content* differs by surface:
228	
229	- **Commit bodies** — bullets are file-by-file: one bullet per file
230	  changed, file plus a one-line gist (e.g.
231	  `README.md: new Overview intro`). Source of truth for the
232	  mechanical edit list.
233	- **Chores / todo / done** — bullets are conceptual (design points,
234	  structural notes, the "what landed and why" at a notch above
235	  file-list granularity). Never a copy of the commit's edit list —
236	  see [Chores section content](#chores-section-content--no-edit-list-git-is-the-record).
237	- **Doc comments** — bullets are whatever structure fits (fields,
238	  cases, invariants).
239	
240	### Problem + plan shape
241	
242	`## In Progress` cycle blocks, chores section intros, and `## Todo`
243	entries use a sharper form of the same shape:
244	
245	- **Problem statement** (the why) — one or two sentences;
246	  don't pad with intent, don't restate the plan.
247	- **Plan bullets** (the what/when) — formality differs by
248	  surface:
249	  - In Progress / chores: a committed ladder, one step per
250	    commit — see
251	    [Conventional-commit shape](#conventional-commit-shape-ladder--chores--commit)
252	    for the per-step title + `(current)` / `(done)` form.
253	  - Todo entries: rough informal bullets, no numbering;
254	    formalized only when the entry is picked up into a
255	    cycle.
256	
257	### Semicolons inside bullets
258	
259	A bullet that joins multiple clauses with semicolons (`A; B; C`)
260	is a list hiding inside running prose — break the clauses into
261	sub-bullets so the structure shows. Semicolons in running prose
262	(intro paragraphs, sentence-joins) are fine. Not absolute: very
263	short clauses or tight pairs can stay joined inside a bullet when
264	breaking would be more noise than signal.
265	
266	### Conventional-commit shape (ladder / chores / commit)
267	
268	A ladder step, its chores section, and its commit description
269	share a *title* shape — a
270	[Conventional Commits](https://www.conventionalcommits.org/en/v1.0.0/)
271	title over [Prose form](#prose-form) detail. They differ in the
272	title's prefix / marker (below) and in bullet *content* — commit
273	bodies are file-by-file, ladder / chores conceptual (see "Bullet
274	*content* differs by surface" above). The shared template:
275	
276	```
277	<optional - X.Y.Z[-N]> <title>   # <title> is the commit's `<type>: <desc>`
278	<optional prose intro>
279	  - <optional item>
280	    <optional prose intro>
281	      - <optional sub-item>
282	      ...
283	```
284	
285	The three surfaces apply it as:
286	
287	- **Ladder step** (`notes/todo.md` `## In Progress`): the title is
288	  prefixed with the step's version — `X.Y.Z-N <title>` — and
289	  carries a `(current)` / `(done)` marker. The bare three-element
290	  `X.Y.Z` (no `-N`) is the close-out step. Detail is bulleted,
291	  never `;`-joined inline.
292	- **Chores section** (`notes/chores/chores-NN.md`): no version
293	  prefix — the `##` header *is* the bare title, with a
294	  `Commits: [[ref]]` line first under it (empty until backfilled —
295	  see [Chores commit references](#chores-commit-references)).
296	- **Commit description**: no version prefix — the title is the
297	  ≤50-col first line; the body is the prose (file-by-file for the
298	  app repo, per [Per-commit flow](notes/cycle-protocol.md#per-commit-flow)).
299	
300	The title is **identical** across all three for a given step, so a
301	step's ladder entry, its chores `##` header, and its commit title
302	line up verbatim — pick the commit title first and reuse it.
303	
304	## Notes file conventions
305	
306	Conventions the bot follows when writing notes files
307	(`notes/todo.md`, `notes/todo-backlog.md`, `notes/bugs.md`,
308	`notes/chores/chores-NN.md`, `notes/done.md`). One source
309	of truth lives here; [`notes/README.md`](notes/README.md)
310	points back.
311	
312	### Notes references
313	
314	Reference *citations* are double-bracketed so the brackets render
315	— `[[N]]`, or `[[2]],[[3]]` for several (comma-separated, not
316	`[2,3]` or `[[2]][[3]]`). The `[N]:` definitions in a file's
317	`# References` section and inline `[text](url)` / `[text](#anchor)`
318	links stay single-bracketed.
319	
320	### Reference numbering
321	
322	Every note file (`todo.md`, `todo-backlog.md`, `bugs.md`,
323	`chores-NN.md`, `done.md`) keeps a file-local `# References`
324	section at the bottom. Reference numbers are scoped to that
325	file — `[1]` in `chores-07.md` and `[1]` in `chores-01.md`
326	are independent slots that may point at completely different
327	URLs. New chores files start their numbering at `[1]`.
328	
329	Treat `[N]` like a **footnote**: the number is a local slot, only
330	meaningful within its file's `# References`. So a `[N]` *citation*
331	(bare `[N]`, or doubled `[[N]]`) never reuses another file's number; to cite a target a sibling
332	file references, pick your own next-local slot and define it
333	(the same target may carry a different number in each file). A
334	`[N]` *inside a code span* (`` `[72]` ``) is different — that's
335	a quoted identifier: literal text naming a ref-key (often from
336	another file's namespace), data, not a citation; it needs no
337	definition here. To point at a section of another file from
338	prose, use an inline link with an anchor —
339	`[that section](../chores-07.md#…)` — not a bare number.
340	
341	A `chores-NN.md` `# References` entry is usually a
342	`/notes/<file>.md#anchor` (or `/ARCHITECTURE.md`) path, but may
343	also be a **commit reference** —
344	`[N]: <commit-url-with-12-hex-SHA> "<full-40-hex-SHA>"`, cited by
345	a section's `Commits:` line. See
346	[Chores commit references](#chores-commit-references) for the
347	why and the exact shape.
348	
349	A file's `# References` can be **re-packed** to a contiguous
350	`[1]..[N]` in first-citation-appearance order — walk the file's
351	prose in document order (`todo.md`: `## Todo` then `## Done`;
352	`chores-NN.md`: top to bottom) and number refs as their first
353	`[[N]]` citation appears. This is a file-local rewrite — only
354	that file's `[[N]]` citations and `[N]:` definitions move; every
355	target and sibling file is untouched. A `[[N]]` inside a `` ` ``
356	code span is a literal token, not a citation, and is left alone.
357	Do it opportunistically (when the namespace has drifted enough to
358	annoy), not on a schedule: `todo.md` fragments fastest (entries
359	land and get pruned every cycle) and is the usual candidate;
360	`chores-NN.md` / `done.md` are append-mostly and only need it
361	after an unusual event (e.g. a bulk retrofit that allocated slots
362	out of document order).
363	
364	### Markdown anchor links
365	
366	GitHub anchor algorithm: lowercase, strip non-alphanumeric
367	characters in place, map remaining spaces to hyphens 1-for-1. Do
368	**not** collapse adjacent whitespace — so `a + b` → `a--b` (spaces
369	on both sides of `+`), but `a: b` → `a-b` (only trailing space on
370	`:`). General markdown reference:
371	[markdownguide.org](https://www.markdownguide.org). GitHub
372	publishes no official spec for auto-generated anchors; the
373	de-facto reference implementation is
374	[github-slugger](https://github.com/Flet/github-slugger).
375	
376	### Todo format
377	
378	`todo.md` is organized into `## In Progress`, `## Todo`
379	(strict priority rank, #1 highest; long-tail backlog in
380	[todo-backlog.md](notes/todo-backlog.md)), `## Ideas`,
381	`## Bugs` (pointer to [bugs.md](notes/bugs.md)), and
382	`## Done` sections. Each item is a short description with
383	reference links to more detail.
384	
385	`## Todo` and `## Bugs` entries carry explicit `1.` `2.` …
386	numbers in the source. For `## Todo` the number is its
387	**priority rank** (#1 highest, descending); for `## Bugs`
388	it's just an index. They're for grepping and at-a-glance
389	"let's do #1" — **not stable IDs**: reorder (to
390	reprioritize), insert, or delete freely, then
391	`vc-x1 fix-todo --no-dry-run` renumbers and normalizes
392	continuation-line indent, so any given number is positional.
393	**To refer to a Todo durably, name it by its title — a
394	plain, greppable text mention.** Not its number (positional,
395	renumbered) and not a markdown link: a numbered list item has
396	no anchor to link to.
397	
398	Numbering helps a human orient in a long list but makes links
399	difficult and fragile — especially an external reference
400	pointing in, which can't be auto-fixed when the list
401	renumbers. A robust fix (a number-free anchor, or a
402	number-tolerant dereference that matches the title slug and
403	wildcards the numeric prefix — a GitHub slug like `5-foo` is
404	encoded, not opaque, so the title is recoverable) is a
405	`validate-numbering` design question, out of scope here.
406	
407	`vc-x1 fix-todo` alone only previews; `vc-x1 validate-todo`
408	is the read-only check. The `## Done` section keeps `-`
409	bullets — items aren't referenced by number once completed.
410	
411	Example shape:
412	
413	```
414	# Todo
415	- Add new feature X [details](features.md#feature-x)
416	- Fix bug Y [[1]]
417	
418	# Done
419	- Fixed issue Z [[2]],[[3]]
420	
421	[1]: bugs.md#bug-y
422	[2]: issues.md#issue-z
423	[3]: fixes.md#fix-z
424	```
425	
426	### Retiring Done entries
427	
428	`todo.md`'s `## Done` section is a rolling buffer of recently
429	shipped work, not a permanent log. Move entries into `done.md`
430	at two natural beats:
431	
432	- **Closing a ladder** — when the final `X.Y.Z` (no suffix)
433	  commit ships, decide which prior entries are no longer
434	  needed for nearby context and migrate them.
435	- **Opening a new ladder** — at `X.Y.Z-0`, do the same sweep
436	  before bumping the version.
437	
438	Migration mechanics:
439	
440	- Move the bullet itself from `todo.md > ## Done` to
441	  `done.md` (preserving the original ref number).
442	- Copy any references the moved entries cite into
443	  `done.md`'s `# References` section (those refs are
444	  file-local, so coexisting with `todo.md`'s namespace is
445	  fine).
446	- Prune any references in `todo.md > # References` no longer
447	  cited by anything in `## In Progress` / `## Todo` /
448	  `## Done`. This frees the numbers for future reuse.
449	
450	## Chores conventions
451	
452	### Headings and entries that record a commit
453	
454	A commit's title is reused verbatim across its records — see
455	[Conventional-commit shape](#conventional-commit-shape-ladder--chores--commit)
456	for the rule. Beyond the chores `##` header, that same string is
457	used for the matching `todo.md > ## Done` entry and any `[N]`
458	reference to that section. Titles carry **no `(<version>)`
459	suffix** — see
460	[Commit description](notes/cycle-protocol.md#commit-description)
461	for why.
462	E.g. the chores header `## refactor: extract config loader`
463	and the Done line `- refactor: extract config loader [[3]]`.
464	The `## Done` entry uses the close-out commit's title.
465	
466	This does **not** apply to organizational headings (`## Todo`,
467	`## In Progress`, `# References`) or to design `###` subsections
468	inside a chores section — those are named for whatever fits.
469	Among the commit-recording ones, exact match is the strong
470	default (nothing absolute): a near-miss just makes it harder to
471	line a record up with its commit.
472	
473	A commit-recording header is provisional while the work is in
474	progress; the *last* edit before `vc-x1 push` syncs it — and the
475	`## Done` entry / `[N]` anchor for that commit — to the final
476	commit title. See [Markdown anchor links](#markdown-anchor-links)
477	for the slug algorithm; the pre-commit checklist catches a
478	dangling `#anchor`, and a future `vc-x1 validate-repo` should too
479	(and should verify the recorded title matches the commit).
480	
481	Any pre-existing sections and `## Done` entries that predate
482	this convention keep their free-form text; the convention
483	applies going forward.
484	
485	### Chores section content — no edit list; git is the record
486	
487	A chores section is: a `Commits:` line (first line under the
488	header — see below), then [Prose form](#prose-form) (intro +
489	bullets) for what landed and why, and any `###` design
490	subsections. Bullets here are **conceptual** — design points,
491	structural notes — never a per-file edit list. That lives in the
492	commit message body, which is the source of truth for "what
493	changed mechanically" (immutable, `git show`-able, naturally
494	scoped to the commit). The chores section is the source of truth
495	for the design thinking; the two cross-link, neither restates the
496	other.
497	
498	When the intro starts wanting to explain a mechanism,
499	hypothesis, or wrinkle, don't inflate it — promote that to its
500	own `###` subsection inside the same `chores-NN.md`. If the
501	wrinkle is a live design concern (something that *should*
502	change, not just be recorded), also add a `notes/todo.md` item
503	with a `[N]` ref pointing at that subsection (todo→chores is the
504	normal ref direction).
505	
506	**Why:** a chores edit list and the commit body were specified
507	to be the same content in two places — and detail written twice
508	drifts. Git owns the mechanical record; chores owns the
509	narrative; `Commits:` links them.
510	
511	### Chores commit references
512	
513	The first line under a chores section header is a `Commits:`
514	line citing the git commit(s) that section records:
515	
516	```
517	## refactor: extract config loader
518	
519	Commits: [[3]]
520	
521	<intro paragraph...>
522	```
523	
524	`Commits:` uses the file-local `[N]` reference machinery (see
525	[Reference numbering](#reference-numbering)),
526	**double-bracketed** so the brackets render — `Commits: [[3]]`,
527	or `Commits: [[3]],[[5]]` for several. (`[[3]]` shows as a
528	literal `[`, the `[3]` link, then a literal `]`; the inner
529	`[3]` resolves against its `[3]:` definition — CommonMark /
530	GitHub / VS Code all do this.) The `# References` definition
531	puts the **commit URL** as the destination, with the **full
532	40-hex SHA** in the title slot:
533	
534	```
535	[3]: https://github.com/<owner>/<repo>/commit/<12-hex> "<40-hex>"
536	```
537	
538	- The 12-hex short SHA in the URL keeps it short; GitHub /
539	  GitLab resolve a unique prefix to the canonical commit page
540	  (GitLab's path has a `/-/` before `commit/`).
541	- The full SHA in the title is host-agnostic and unambiguous —
542	  it survives a repo host change, `git show <40-hex>` works in
543	  any clone, and external tooling scraping the notes (a
544	  database, say) gets the canonical identifier.
545	
546	**Timing.** A commit's SHA isn't stable until it lands on a
547	**permanent branch** (`main`, or a long-lived release/patch
548	branch) — a rebase or squash rewrites it on the way. A commit
549	can't record its own SHA, so the fill lands one push later:
550	every section opens with an **empty `Commits:`**, and each push
551	backfills the `Commits:` of the commits the previous push made
552	permanent. On a topic branch a section waits until the branch
553	lands. The commit itself is the record, and
554	`git log --grep "<title>"` finds it. See cycle-protocol
555	[Commits backfill](notes/cycle-protocol.md#commits-backfill).
556	
557	## Cycle Protocol
558	
559	Every change runs as a **cycle**: Preparation (`X.Y.Z-0`) →
560	Work commits (`X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, …) → Close-out (bare
561	`X.Y.Z`). Each commit runs through a per-commit flow whose
562	cargo cycle is fmt → clippy → test → install (exact commands
563	in cycle-protocol.md; skip-able for purely-docs commits,
564	mandatory at close-out). The full protocol — numbering, per-commit
565	flow, reviewing changes, close-out, pushing, ochid
566	trailers, sub-cycles — lives in
567	[`notes/cycle-protocol.md`](notes/cycle-protocol.md). Read
568	it before any commit work — and before any push, cycle or
569	not: pushes always need per-push user approval (see
570	[Pushing policy](notes/cycle-protocol.md#policy)).
571	
572	**Hard stop after push/finalize.** Once a push or `.claude`
573	finalize is invoked (`vc-x1 push` does both), emit
574	**nothing** — no tool call, no text, no verification, no
575	summary — until the user speaks. Closing words go *before*
576	the invoke; post-push verification happens next turn at the
577	user's direction. See
578	[After push or finalize](notes/cycle-protocol.md#after-push-or-finalize-stop-and-wait).
579	
580	**`vc-x1 push` behaviors to keep in mind.** Two, independent
581	of project language:
582	
583	- **Preflight** runs the cargo cycle (fmt → clippy → test). On
584	  a cargo project it works as-is, so `vc-x1 push <bookmark>`
585	  runs it before the review gate. On a project with no cargo
586	  cycle — or any time preflight can't run — invoke `vc-x1 push
587	  <bookmark> --from message ...` to skip preflight and the
588	  interactive review gate, doing the diff review in
589	  conversation first.
590	- **ochid trailers** are injected by `vc-x1 push` itself —
591	  don't hand-write them into the commit body or
592	  `--title`/`--body`.
593	
594	**Python/uv.** Where a Python/uv project (no cargo) departs
595	from the general protocol:
596	
597	- No cargo cycle, so the no-cargo case above is the norm —
598	  always push with `vc-x1 push <bookmark> --from message ...`.
599	- Version strings are PEP 440, whose local segment can't carry
600	  a `-`: the ladder/chores/todo notation `X.Y.Z-N.M` becomes
601	  `X.Y.Z+N.M` in `fc-config.toml` `[project].version` (e.g.
602	  `0.3.0-5.3.0` -> `0.3.0+5.3.0`). Hyphen when we talk, `+` in
603	  the version string.
604	
605	
606	## Code Conventions
607	
608	### Doc comments on every file, function, and method
609	
610	Every `.rs` file must begin with a `//!` module docstring. Every
611	function and method must have a `///` doc comment. Keep them brief —
612	one sentence of purpose is often enough; the discipline is that the
613	comment exists, not that it be long. Doc comments follow the
614	[Prose form](#prose-form) shape (intro + bullets).
615	
616	This is a deliberate override of the generic "write no comments"
617	default that applies to inline `//` comments. Doc comments on the
618	module / item surface are expected; inline explanatory comments
619	inside function bodies remain discouraged unless they capture a
620	non-obvious WHY.
621	
622	**Clap-derive args:** doc comments on `#[arg(...)]` fields drive
623	`--help` output. Clap reflows by default and collapses bullets
624	into running prose. Add `#[arg(verbatim_doc_comment, ...)]` on
625	any field whose doc comment uses bullets so each `- …` lands on
626	its own line in the rendered help.
627	
628	### `// OK: …` comments on `unwrap*` calls (Rust)
629	
630	Non-test code that calls `.unwrap()`, `.unwrap_or(…)`,
631	`.unwrap_or_default()`, or `.unwrap_or_else(…)` must have a trailing
632	`// OK: …` comment that justifies why the call is acceptable.
633	
634	- `// OK: <specific reason>` — document the real precondition,
635	  invariant, or domain reason. Preferred whenever the reason isn't
636	  self-evident.
637	- `// OK: obvious` — the default is self-evident from context (e.g.
638	  `desc.lines().next().unwrap_or("")` — empty desc → empty title).
639	
640	Bare `// OK` is not used (reads like a truncated comment).
641	Abbreviations (e.g. `SE`) are not used because they require a decoder
642	ring for readers seeing the code out of context.
643	
644	For provably-unreachable `.unwrap()` calls, also prefix with
645	`#[allow(clippy::unwrap_used)]` so the site stays silent if we enable
646	the project-wide `clippy::unwrap_used` lint later.
647	
648	```rust
649	let max = stderr_level.unwrap_or(LevelFilter::Info); // OK: default verbosity when -v/-vv absent
650	let first_line = desc.lines().next().unwrap_or("");  // OK: obvious
651	
652	match matches.len() {
653	    1 => {
654	        #[allow(clippy::unwrap_used)]
655	        // OK: `1 =>` arm guarantees matches.len() == 1
656	        Ok(TitleMatch::One(matches.into_iter().next().unwrap()))
657	    }
658	    // ...
659	}
660	```
661	
662	Tests (`#[cfg(test)]`) are exempt — panicking on setup failure is the
663	correct test behavior.
664	
665	### Ask for clarification on ambiguous input
666	
667	When user input is ambiguous or missing necessary detail, stop and
668	ask a specific question. Do not proceed on a guess and hope the
669	result lands right — a clarifying question costs a few seconds;
670	redoing misaligned work costs much more.
671	
672	### Recognize when stuck
673	
674	If a simple task has eaten 5+ minutes of thinking or back-and-forth
675	without progress, stop. Summarize what's blocking — unclear
676	requirements, unfamiliar API surface, conflicting signals — and ask.
677	Continued flailing produces worse outcomes than a direct "I'm stuck
678	on X."
679

# AGENTS.md - Bot Instructions

## Project Structure

This project uses **two separate jj-git repos**:

1. **App repo** (`/` — project root): Contains the application source code.
2. **Bot session repo** (`/.claude/`): Contains Claude Code session data.

Both repos are managed with `jj` (Jujutsu), which coexists with git.

**Committing vs pushing.** Default to `vc-x1 push` — it commits and
publishes both repos together, each carrying one `ochid:` pointing at
the other (see [Cycle Protocol](#cycle-protocol) and
[ochid trailers](#cross-repo-linking-ochid-trailers)). Use a bare
`jj commit` (see [jj Basics](#jj-basics)) only when:

- the change will never be published — no `ochid:`;
- the commit will be squashed away before its series is pushed
  (loop-and-squash) — no `ochid:`; it disappears in the squash;
- the commit will be pushed later as a non-top commit — `jj commit`
  and add its `ochid:` now, because `vc-x1 push` stamps only the
  topmost commit it pushes, never the ancestors.

## Repo Paths (relative from project root)

- App repo: `.` (project root)
- Bot session repo: `.claude`
  (symlink from `~/.claude/projects/<path-to-project-root>/.claude`)

## Working Directory

Prefer staying in the project root. Use `-R` flags or absolute paths
to target other directories rather than `cd`. If `cd` seems necessary,
discuss with the user first — losing track of cwd causes subtle
command failures downstream.

When invoking shell commands, prefer the shortest unambiguous path —
usually relative to cwd (`ls notes/`, not
`ls /home/wink/data/prgs/rust/vc-x1/notes/`). Long absolute paths
clutter the transcript without adding information. Out-of-workspace
paths (`/tmp/...`, `~/.config/...`) stay absolute. Read/Edit/Write
tool args also stay absolute — that's a tool-boundary constraint,
not a stylistic choice.

Run **one command per shell invocation** — don't bundle several
steps into a single compound script (`a && b; c`). Bundling hides
which step produced which output and makes a failure ambiguous;
executing one at a time keeps the details visible and reviewable.
Exceptions are a genuine pipeline (`grep | sort`) or a tight,
inseparable pair where the join is the point.

## File reads — read the slice you need

Long notes files are appended to over time. Read only the
slice your task needs; grep or read further on demand.

- **`notes/todo.md`** (the routine acquaint read) — first
  ~60 lines covers intro + `## In Progress` + the top of the
  ranked `## Todo` (priorities, #1 highest). `Read` with
  `offset=0, limit=60`. `## Ideas` sits below `## Todo`; read
  further only when chasing a lower-ranked entry, an Idea, a
  `[N]` ref, or auditing the whole list.
- **`notes/todo-backlog.md`** — the long-tail backlog
  (lower-priority entries below the ranked `## Todo`). Read
  only when picking up a backlog item; grep to locate it
  first.
- **`notes/bugs.md`** — the bug list. Small; read whole
  when triaging a bug or chasing the `## Bugs` pointer in
  todo.md.
- **`notes/done.md`** + **`notes/chores/chores-NN.md`** —
  historical / append-mostly. Scan headings first
  (`grep '^## ' notes/chores/chores-NN.md`), then read only
  the section you need.

**Why:** the routine read should stay small. `notes/todo.md`
grows every cycle, so the backlog and bugs live in sibling
files rather than inline; the same "slice you need" rule
applies to historical files.

## Memory

Do not use the bot's per-project memory directory
(`~/.claude/projects/<path>/memory/`). In a dual-repo setup with
AGENTS.md it provides no capability AGENTS.md doesn't already cover,
and it loses on discoverability:

- **AGENTS.md** — at the repo root, a well-known location for bot
  instructions, committed, reviewable, visible to every collaborator
  (human or bot).
- **Memory directory** — hidden under the user's home, tied to one
  machine, invisible to anyone but the bot, never diffed or reviewed.

Easy for everyone to find beats convenient for the bot alone. Put
durable context in AGENTS.md (or committed `notes/`) instead.

## Speculation marker

Durable text the bot writes — AGENTS.md, `notes/`, commit bodies,
chores sections — should stick to observations and direct descriptions
of the code or data. If a mechanism, hypothesis, or causal claim
enters the text, prefix it with "We think ..." (a royal "we") so a
reader can tell the measured from the inferred.

**Why:** unmarked speculation reads like evidence, and a future reader
(or the bot on a later session) can pick it up as a known fact when
it's not. Measured / inferred is a distinction worth keeping visible
in the written record.

**How to apply:** observations and factual descriptions need no
marker. Prefix with "We think ..." (or a close variant like
"Our guess is ...") when the claim is a mechanism ("X wins
because Y caches better"), a cause ("the drift was due to thermal
state"), a prediction ("this should scale linearly"), or any
reasoning not directly supported by the data on hand.

## jj Basics

**Use jj, not git, for version-control operations** (status, log,
diff, commit, push, history rewrite). jj coexists with the git
backend, so the repo *can* be driven with raw `git`, but this
project's workflow — bookmarks, the working-copy `@` model, ochid
trailers — is expressed in jj terms; reaching for `git` invites
state that doesn't match the jj documentation here. There is no
`jj mv`: to move/rename a tracked file, just `mv` it on disk and
jj detects the rename by content.

- `jj st -R .` / `jj st -R .claude` — show working copy status
- `jj log -R .` / `jj log -R .claude` — show commit log
- `jj commit -m "title" -m "body" -R <repo>` — finalize working copy into a commit
- `jj describe -m "title" -m "body" -R <repo>` — set description without committing
- `jj git push --bookmark <name> -R <repo>` — push a bookmark (no
  `--allow-new` flag; jj pushes new bookmarks without special flags)
- In jj, the working copy (@) is always a mutable commit being edited.
  `jj commit` finalizes it and creates a new empty working copy on top.
- The `.claude` repo always has uncommitted changes during an active
  session because session data updates continuously.
- `jj rebase` uses `--onto`/`-o` to name the destination(s).

## Cross-repo linking (ochid trailers)

The cross-reference between the app repo and the `.claude` bot
repo is what makes the dual-repo work: every commit points at its
counterpart in the other repo, so the "what" (code) and the
"why / how" (session) stay linked across time. That pointer is the
**ochid** (Other Change ID) git trailer.

A **chid** is jj's change ID — a permanent identifier that
survives rebases and `describe`s (unlike the commit ID / git SHA,
which changes on rewrite). An **ochid** trailer carries the
counterpart commit's chid as a workspace-root-relative path:

- Paths start with `/` — the workspace root, i.e. the app repo
  (vc-template-x1). `/.claude` is the bot session sub-repo.
- `ochid: /<chid>` references a change in the **app repo**.
- `ochid: /.claude/<chid>` references a change in the **`.claude`
  repo**.

Trailers are blank-line-separated `key: value` lines at the end of
the commit body, using the chid's **12-character** prefix:

```
ochid: /.claude/xvzvruqowktp   # points to a .claude repo change
ochid: /wtpmottvxqzl           # points to an app repo change
```

How many, and which direction:

- **Code-side commits** each carry one
  `ochid: /.claude/<.claude-chid>` — the `.claude` change ID.
- **The `.claude` commit** carries one `ochid: /<code-chid>` per
  code commit in that push. More than one occurs on a Merge
  non-ff close-out (one ochid per Work commit in the cycle).

Use `vc-x1 chid -s code,bot -L` to capture the change IDs (first
line app, second `.claude`).

### Resolvability

A change ID travels with its commit: a **pushed** commit resolves
to the same chid in every clone — cloning the `.claude` repo gave
the published `main` tip the same chid as an existing clone. We
think jj carries the change ID in the git commit object, so it
survives `jj git clone` / fetch.

The local-only case is the **working-copy `@`**: jj mints a fresh
random chid for `@` in each clone, so an unpushed `@` is never a
stable ochid target. This is why a `.claude` ochid names `@-`
(the last committed change), not `@`.

### .vc-config.toml

Each repo contains a `.vc-config.toml` that identifies its
location within the workspace, so tools resolve these paths
without repeating the workspace path in every trailer:

```toml
# In vc-template-x1 (workspace root):
[workspace]
path = "/"

# In .claude (sub-repo):
[workspace]
path = "/.claude"
```

## Prose form

Long-lived prose on this project follows one basic shape: a short intro
that explains the *why* or the high-level *what*, then a `-` bullet
list for the details. Wrap lines at ≤72 cols (bullet continuations
indent two spaces). Avoid wall-of-prose paragraphs — they hide the
structure that bullets make scannable.

Surfaces that use this shape:

- Module / function / struct / field doc comments in `.rs` files —
  see [Doc comments](#doc-comments-on-every-file-function-and-method).
- Commit message bodies (both app-repo and session-repo). The
  ≤50-col title is the commit-specific add-on; see
  [Per-commit flow](notes/cycle-protocol.md#per-commit-flow).
- Chore descriptions in `notes/chores/chores-NN.md` — see
  [Chores section content](#chores-section-content--no-edit-list-git-is-the-record).
- Todo and Done entries in `notes/todo.md` when an entry needs more
  than one line of detail. Pure one-liners are still fine.

Bullet *content* differs by surface:

- **Commit bodies** — bullets are file-by-file: one bullet per file
  changed, file plus a one-line gist (e.g.
  `README.md: new Overview intro`). Source of truth for the
  mechanical edit list.
- **Chores / todo / done** — bullets are conceptual (design points,
  structural notes, the "what landed and why" at a notch above
  file-list granularity). Never a copy of the commit's edit list —
  see [Chores section content](#chores-section-content--no-edit-list-git-is-the-record).
- **Doc comments** — bullets are whatever structure fits (fields,
  cases, invariants).

### Problem + plan shape

`## In Progress` cycle blocks, chores section intros, and `## Todo`
entries use a sharper form of the same shape:

- **Problem statement** (the why) — one or two sentences;
  don't pad with intent, don't restate the plan.
- **Plan bullets** (the what/when) — formality differs by
  surface:
  - In Progress / chores: a committed ladder, one step per
    commit — see
    [Conventional-commit shape](#conventional-commit-shape-ladder--chores--commit)
    for the per-step title + `(current)` / `(done)` form.
  - Todo entries: rough informal bullets, no numbering;
    formalized only when the entry is picked up into a
    cycle.

### Semicolons inside bullets

A bullet that joins multiple clauses with semicolons (`A; B; C`)
is a list hiding inside running prose — break the clauses into
sub-bullets so the structure shows. Semicolons in running prose
(intro paragraphs, sentence-joins) are fine. Not absolute: very
short clauses or tight pairs can stay joined inside a bullet when
breaking would be more noise than signal.

### Conventional-commit shape (ladder / chores / commit)

A ladder step, its chores section, and its commit description
share a *title* shape — a
[Conventional Commits](https://www.conventionalcommits.org/en/v1.0.0/)
title over [Prose form](#prose-form) detail. They differ in the
title's prefix / marker (below) and in bullet *content* — commit
bodies are file-by-file, ladder / chores conceptual (see "Bullet
*content* differs by surface" above). The shared template:

```
<optional - X.Y.Z[-N]> <title>   # <title> is the commit's `<type>: <desc>`
<optional prose intro>
  - <optional item>
    <optional prose intro>
      - <optional sub-item>
      ...
```

The three surfaces apply it as:

- **Ladder step** (`notes/todo.md` `## In Progress`): the title is
  prefixed with the step's version — `X.Y.Z-N <title>` — and
  carries a `(current)` / `(done)` marker. The bare three-element
  `X.Y.Z` (no `-N`) is the close-out step. Detail is bulleted,
  never `;`-joined inline.
- **Chores section** (`notes/chores/chores-NN.md`): no version
  prefix — the `##` header *is* the bare title, with a
  `Commits: [[ref]]` line first under it (empty until backfilled —
  see [Chores commit references](#chores-commit-references)).
- **Commit description**: no version prefix — the title is the
  ≤50-col first line; the body is the prose (file-by-file for the
  app repo, per [Per-commit flow](notes/cycle-protocol.md#per-commit-flow)).

The title is **identical** across all three for a given step, so a
step's ladder entry, its chores `##` header, and its commit title
line up verbatim — pick the commit title first and reuse it.

## Notes file conventions

Conventions the bot follows when writing notes files
(`notes/todo.md`, `notes/todo-backlog.md`, `notes/bugs.md`,
`notes/chores/chores-NN.md`, `notes/done.md`). One source
of truth lives here; [`notes/README.md`](notes/README.md)
points back.

### Notes references

Reference *citations* are double-bracketed so the brackets render
— `[[N]]`, or `[[2]],[[3]]` for several (comma-separated, not
`[2,3]` or `[[2]][[3]]`). The `[N]:` definitions in a file's
`# References` section and inline `[text](url)` / `[text](#anchor)`
links stay single-bracketed.

### Reference numbering

Every note file (`todo.md`, `todo-backlog.md`, `bugs.md`,
`chores-NN.md`, `done.md`) keeps a file-local `# References`
section at the bottom. Reference numbers are scoped to that
file — `[1]` in `chores-07.md` and `[1]` in `chores-01.md`
are independent slots that may point at completely different
URLs. New chores files start their numbering at `[1]`.

Treat `[N]` like a **footnote**: the number is a local slot, only
meaningful within its file's `# References`. So a `[N]` *citation*
(bare `[N]`, or doubled `[[N]]`) never reuses another file's number; to cite a target a sibling
file references, pick your own next-local slot and define it
(the same target may carry a different number in each file). A
`[N]` *inside a code span* (`` `[72]` ``) is different — that's
a quoted identifier: literal text naming a ref-key (often from
another file's namespace), data, not a citation; it needs no
definition here. To point at a section of another file from
prose, use an inline link with an anchor —
`[that section](../chores-07.md#…)` — not a bare number.

A `chores-NN.md` `# References` entry is usually a
`/notes/<file>.md#anchor` (or `/ARCHITECTURE.md`) path, but may
also be a **commit reference** —
`[N]: <commit-url-with-12-hex-SHA> "<full-40-hex-SHA>"`, cited by
a section's `Commits:` line. See
[Chores commit references](#chores-commit-references) for the
why and the exact shape.

A file's `# References` can be **re-packed** to a contiguous
`[1]..[N]` in first-citation-appearance order — walk the file's
prose in document order (`todo.md`: `## Todo` then `## Done`;
`chores-NN.md`: top to bottom) and number refs as their first
`[[N]]` citation appears. This is a file-local rewrite — only
that file's `[[N]]` citations and `[N]:` definitions move; every
target and sibling file is untouched. A `[[N]]` inside a `` ` ``
code span is a literal token, not a citation, and is left alone.
Do it opportunistically (when the namespace has drifted enough to
annoy), not on a schedule: `todo.md` fragments fastest (entries
land and get pruned every cycle) and is the usual candidate;
`chores-NN.md` / `done.md` are append-mostly and only need it
after an unusual event (e.g. a bulk retrofit that allocated slots
out of document order).

### Markdown anchor links

GitHub anchor algorithm: lowercase, strip non-alphanumeric
characters in place, map remaining spaces to hyphens 1-for-1. Do
**not** collapse adjacent whitespace — so `a + b` → `a--b` (spaces
on both sides of `+`), but `a: b` → `a-b` (only trailing space on
`:`). General markdown reference:
[markdownguide.org](https://www.markdownguide.org). GitHub
publishes no official spec for auto-generated anchors; the
de-facto reference implementation is
[github-slugger](https://github.com/Flet/github-slugger).

### Todo format

`todo.md` is organized into `## In Progress`, `## Todo`
(strict priority rank, #1 highest; long-tail backlog in
[todo-backlog.md](notes/todo-backlog.md)), `## Ideas`,
`## Bugs` (pointer to [bugs.md](notes/bugs.md)), and
`## Done` sections. Each item is a short description with
reference links to more detail.

`## Todo` and `## Bugs` entries carry explicit `1.` `2.` …
numbers in the source. For `## Todo` the number is its
**priority rank** (#1 highest, descending); for `## Bugs`
it's just an index. They're for grepping and at-a-glance
"let's do #1" — **not stable IDs**: reorder (to
reprioritize), insert, or delete freely, then
`vc-x1 fix-todo --no-dry-run` renumbers and normalizes
continuation-line indent, so any given number is positional.
**To refer to a Todo durably, name it by its title — a
plain, greppable text mention.** Not its number (positional,
renumbered) and not a markdown link: a numbered list item has
no anchor to link to.

Numbering helps a human orient in a long list but makes links
difficult and fragile — especially an external reference
pointing in, which can't be auto-fixed when the list
renumbers. A robust fix (a number-free anchor, or a
number-tolerant dereference that matches the title slug and
wildcards the numeric prefix — a GitHub slug like `5-foo` is
encoded, not opaque, so the title is recoverable) is a
`validate-numbering` design question, out of scope here.

`vc-x1 fix-todo` alone only previews; `vc-x1 validate-todo`
is the read-only check. The `## Done` section keeps `-`
bullets — items aren't referenced by number once completed.

Example shape:

```
# Todo
- Add new feature X [details](features.md#feature-x)
- Fix bug Y [[1]]

# Done
- Fixed issue Z [[2]],[[3]]

[1]: bugs.md#bug-y
[2]: issues.md#issue-z
[3]: fixes.md#fix-z
```

### Retiring Done entries

`todo.md`'s `## Done` section is a rolling buffer of recently
shipped work, not a permanent log. Move entries into `done.md`
at two natural beats:

- **Closing a ladder** — when the final `X.Y.Z` (no suffix)
  commit ships, decide which prior entries are no longer
  needed for nearby context and migrate them.
- **Opening a new ladder** — at `X.Y.Z-0`, do the same sweep
  before bumping the version.

Migration mechanics:

- Move the bullet itself from `todo.md > ## Done` to
  `done.md` (preserving the original ref number).
- Copy any references the moved entries cite into
  `done.md`'s `# References` section (those refs are
  file-local, so coexisting with `todo.md`'s namespace is
  fine).
- Prune any references in `todo.md > # References` no longer
  cited by anything in `## In Progress` / `## Todo` /
  `## Done`. This frees the numbers for future reuse.

## Chores conventions

### Headings and entries that record a commit

A commit's title is reused verbatim across its records — see
[Conventional-commit shape](#conventional-commit-shape-ladder--chores--commit)
for the rule. Beyond the chores `##` header, that same string is
used for the matching `todo.md > ## Done` entry and any `[N]`
reference to that section. Titles carry **no `(<version>)`
suffix** — see
[Commit description](notes/cycle-protocol.md#commit-description)
for why.
E.g. the chores header `## refactor: extract config loader`
and the Done line `- refactor: extract config loader [[3]]`.
The `## Done` entry uses the close-out commit's title.

This does **not** apply to organizational headings (`## Todo`,
`## In Progress`, `# References`) or to design `###` subsections
inside a chores section — those are named for whatever fits.
Among the commit-recording ones, exact match is the strong
default (nothing absolute): a near-miss just makes it harder to
line a record up with its commit.

A commit-recording header is provisional while the work is in
progress; the *last* edit before `vc-x1 push` syncs it — and the
`## Done` entry / `[N]` anchor for that commit — to the final
commit title. See [Markdown anchor links](#markdown-anchor-links)
for the slug algorithm; the pre-commit checklist catches a
dangling `#anchor`, and a future `vc-x1 validate-repo` should too
(and should verify the recorded title matches the commit).

Any pre-existing sections and `## Done` entries that predate
this convention keep their free-form text; the convention
applies going forward.

### Chores section content — no edit list; git is the record

A chores section is: a `Commits:` line (first line under the
header — see below), then [Prose form](#prose-form) (intro +
bullets) for what landed and why, and any `###` design
subsections. Bullets here are **conceptual** — design points,
structural notes — never a per-file edit list. That lives in the
commit message body, which is the source of truth for "what
changed mechanically" (immutable, `git show`-able, naturally
scoped to the commit). The chores section is the source of truth
for the design thinking; the two cross-link, neither restates the
other.

When the intro starts wanting to explain a mechanism,
hypothesis, or wrinkle, don't inflate it — promote that to its
own `###` subsection inside the same `chores-NN.md`. If the
wrinkle is a live design concern (something that *should*
change, not just be recorded), also add a `notes/todo.md` item
with a `[N]` ref pointing at that subsection (todo→chores is the
normal ref direction).

**Why:** a chores edit list and the commit body were specified
to be the same content in two places — and detail written twice
drifts. Git owns the mechanical record; chores owns the
narrative; `Commits:` links them.

### Chores commit references

The first line under a chores section header is a `Commits:`
line citing the git commit(s) that section records:

```
## refactor: extract config loader

Commits: [[3]]

<intro paragraph...>
```

`Commits:` uses the file-local `[N]` reference machinery (see
[Reference numbering](#reference-numbering)),
**double-bracketed** so the brackets render — `Commits: [[3]]`,
or `Commits: [[3]],[[5]]` for several. (`[[3]]` shows as a
literal `[`, the `[3]` link, then a literal `]`; the inner
`[3]` resolves against its `[3]:` definition — CommonMark /
GitHub / VS Code all do this.) The `# References` definition
puts the **commit URL** as the destination, with the **full
40-hex SHA** in the title slot:

```
[3]: https://github.com/<owner>/<repo>/commit/<12-hex> "<40-hex>"
```

- The 12-hex short SHA in the URL keeps it short; GitHub /
  GitLab resolve a unique prefix to the canonical commit page
  (GitLab's path has a `/-/` before `commit/`).
- The full SHA in the title is host-agnostic and unambiguous —
  it survives a repo host change, `git show <40-hex>` works in
  any clone, and external tooling scraping the notes (a
  database, say) gets the canonical identifier.

**Timing.** A commit's SHA isn't stable until it lands on a
**permanent branch** (`main`, or a long-lived release/patch
branch) — a rebase or squash rewrites it on the way. A commit
can't record its own SHA, so the fill lands one push later:
every section opens with an **empty `Commits:`**, and each push
backfills the `Commits:` of the commits the previous push made
permanent. On a topic branch a section waits until the branch
lands. The commit itself is the record, and
`git log --grep "<title>"` finds it. See cycle-protocol
[Commits backfill](notes/cycle-protocol.md#commits-backfill).

## Cycle Protocol

Every change runs as a **cycle**: Preparation (`X.Y.Z-0`) →
Work commits (`X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, …) → Close-out (bare
`X.Y.Z`). Each commit runs through a per-commit flow whose
cargo cycle is fmt → clippy → test → install (exact commands
in cycle-protocol.md; skip-able for purely-docs commits,
mandatory at close-out). The full protocol — numbering, per-commit
flow, reviewing changes, close-out, pushing, ochid
trailers, sub-cycles — lives in
[`notes/cycle-protocol.md`](notes/cycle-protocol.md). Read
it before any commit work — and before any push, cycle or
not: pushes always need per-push user approval (see
[Pushing policy](notes/cycle-protocol.md#policy)).

**Hard stop after push/finalize.** Once a push or `.claude`
finalize is invoked (`vc-x1 push` does both), emit
**nothing** — no tool call, no text, no verification, no
summary — until the user speaks. Closing words go *before*
the invoke; post-push verification happens next turn at the
user's direction. See
[After push or finalize](notes/cycle-protocol.md#after-push-or-finalize-stop-and-wait).

**`vc-x1 push` behaviors to keep in mind.** Two, independent
of project language:

- **Preflight** runs the cargo cycle (fmt → clippy → test). On
  a cargo project it works as-is, so `vc-x1 push <bookmark>`
  runs it before the review gate. On a project with no cargo
  cycle — or any time preflight can't run — invoke `vc-x1 push
  <bookmark> --from message ...` to skip preflight and the
  interactive review gate, doing the diff review in
  conversation first.
- **ochid trailers** are injected by `vc-x1 push` itself —
  don't hand-write them into the commit body or
  `--title`/`--body`.

**Python/uv.** Where a Python/uv project (no cargo) departs
from the general protocol:

- No cargo cycle, so the no-cargo case above is the norm —
  always push with `vc-x1 push <bookmark> --from message ...`.
- Version strings are PEP 440, whose local segment can't carry
  a `-`: the ladder/chores/todo notation `X.Y.Z-N.M` becomes
  `X.Y.Z+N.M` in `fc-config.toml` `[project].version` (e.g.
  `0.3.0-5.3.0` -> `0.3.0+5.3.0`). Hyphen when we talk, `+` in
  the version string.


## Code Conventions

### Doc comments on every file, function, and method

Every `.rs` file must begin with a `//!` module docstring. Every
function and method must have a `///` doc comment. Keep them brief —
one sentence of purpose is often enough; the discipline is that the
comment exists, not that it be long. Doc comments follow the
[Prose form](#prose-form) shape (intro + bullets).

This is a deliberate override of the generic "write no comments"
default that applies to inline `//` comments. Doc comments on the
module / item surface are expected; inline explanatory comments
inside function bodies remain discouraged unless they capture a
non-obvious WHY.

**Clap-derive args:** doc comments on `#[arg(...)]` fields drive
`--help` output. Clap reflows by default and collapses bullets
into running prose. Add `#[arg(verbatim_doc_comment, ...)]` on
any field whose doc comment uses bullets so each `- …` lands on
its own line in the rendered help.

### `// OK: …` comments on `unwrap*` calls (Rust)

Non-test code that calls `.unwrap()`, `.unwrap_or(…)`,
`.unwrap_or_default()`, or `.unwrap_or_else(…)` must have a trailing
`// OK: …` comment that justifies why the call is acceptable.

- `// OK: <specific reason>` — document the real precondition,
  invariant, or domain reason. Preferred whenever the reason isn't
  self-evident.
- `// OK: obvious` — the default is self-evident from context (e.g.
  `desc.lines().next().unwrap_or("")` — empty desc → empty title).

Bare `// OK` is not used (reads like a truncated comment).
Abbreviations (e.g. `SE`) are not used because they require a decoder
ring for readers seeing the code out of context.

For provably-unreachable `.unwrap()` calls, also prefix with
`#[allow(clippy::unwrap_used)]` so the site stays silent if we enable
the project-wide `clippy::unwrap_used` lint later.

```rust
let max = stderr_level.unwrap_or(LevelFilter::Info); // OK: default verbosity when -v/-vv absent
let first_line = desc.lines().next().unwrap_or("");  // OK: obvious

match matches.len() {
    1 => {
        #[allow(clippy::unwrap_used)]
        // OK: `1 =>` arm guarantees matches.len() == 1
        Ok(TitleMatch::One(matches.into_iter().next().unwrap()))
    }
    // ...
}
```

Tests (`#[cfg(test)]`) are exempt — panicking on setup failure is the
correct test behavior.

### Ask for clarification on ambiguous input

When user input is ambiguous or missing necessary detail, stop and
ask a specific question. Do not proceed on a guess and hope the
result lands right — a clarifying question costs a few seconds;
redoing misaligned work costs much more.

### Recognize when stuck

If a simple task has eaten 5+ minutes of thinking or back-and-forth
without progress, stop. Summarize what's blocking — unclear
requirements, unfamiliar API surface, conflicting signals — and ask.
Continued flailing produces worse outcomes than a direct "I'm stuck
on X."
