content.text: These are substantial — a dual-repo jj workflow with `vc-x1 push` as the publish path, and a cycle protocol governing commits/pushes. Let me read the remaining notes, especially the cycle protocol (it governs pushing) and the chores dir.

content.content:
1	# Cycle protocol
2	
3	This protocol uses [Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). It
4	contains instructions on how a commit cycle is accomplished.
5	
6	## Cycles
7	
8	A cycle has three phases:
9	
10	- **[Preparation](#preparation)** (`X.Y.Z-0`) — the cycle's
11	  first commit. Sets up the cycle:
12	  - Bump `Cargo.toml` to `X.Y.Z-0`.
13	  - Pick up a `## Todo` item (typically the top-ranked,
14	    #1) into `## In Progress` (bold title + succinct problem
15	    statement + plan ladder).
16	  - Open the [chores section](#chores-sections).
17	- **[Work-N](#work-n)** (`X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, …) — the
18	  commits that implement the change. As many as the change
19	  needs; each runs through the
20	  [per-commit flow](#per-commit-flow).
21	- **[Close-out](#close-out)** (bare `X.Y.Z`) — the cycle's
22	  last commit. Bookkeeping only:
23	  - Move the picked-up item to a one-line entry in
24	    `## Done`.
25	  - Move the `## In Progress` block into the
26	    [chores section](#chores-sections).
27	  - Optionally update `notes/README.md` if functionality
28	    changed.
29	
30	A cycle's commits are published to the project remote
31	either incrementally or as one batch at close-out; the
32	result must always be published at close-out. See
33	[Pushing](#pushing).
34	
35	**Sub-cycles.** When a Work commit's scope grows enough to
36	warrant its own ladder, it subdivides — `X.Y.Z-3.0`
37	Preparation, `X.Y.Z-3.1` / `X.Y.Z-3.2` Work, `X.Y.Z-3`
38	Close-out. The same three-phase shape applies recursively
39	at every depth. See [Numbering](#numbering) for the
40	suffix rule and [Sub-cycle ladders](#sub-cycle-ladders)
41	for the local-ladder mechanics.
42	
43	## Chores sections
44	
45	A **chores section** is a `##` section in
46	`notes/chores/chores-NN.md` recording landed work. In
47	general, every commit that lands on the permanent branch
48	should have a reference to it on a `Commits:` list in a
49	chores file.
50	
51	The phrase **"Open" the chores section** means append a
52	`##` header to the current `notes/chores/chores-NN.md`
53	with the title it records (e.g. `## refactor: foo bar`),
54	followed by an **empty `Commits:`** line. The narrative
55	body stays empty until close-out (see [Close-out](#close-out));
56	the `Commits:` line is backfilled later, once the commit is
57	permanent (see [Commits backfill](#commits-backfill) below).
58	
59	Fuller chores conventions (content rules, header sync,
60	design subsection pattern, `Commits:` formatting) live in
61	AGENTS.md [Chores conventions](../AGENTS.md#chores-conventions).
62	
63	### Commits backfill
64	
65	A chores section's `Commits:` line cites the commit(s) it
66	records, by SHA — but a SHA isn't stable until the commit
67	lands on a **permanent branch** (`main`, or a long-lived
68	release/patch branch that won't be rewritten); a rebase or
69	squash rewrites it on the way. So:
70	
71	- A chores section is **opened with an empty `Commits:`** line.
72	- **Backfill once the commit is on a permanent branch**,
73	  where its SHA is final. A commit can't record its own SHA
74	  (that would change the hash), so the fill always lands one
75	  push later: **each push backfills the `Commits:` of the
76	  commits the previous push made permanent.** On a topic
77	  branch the sections instead wait until the branch lands —
78	  so no SHA is ever written that a later rebase could
79	  invalidate.
80	
81	Use `[[N]]` refs — several as `[[N]],[[M]]` only when one
82	section records multiple commits (a merge non-ff close-out) —
83	with the commit URL + 40-hex SHA in the file's `# References`
84	(format in AGENTS.md
85	[Chores commit references](../AGENTS.md#chores-commit-references)).
86	A section's `##` title matches its commit title, so a rare
87	deliberate rewrite of a permanent-branch commit re-syncs via
88	`git log --grep "<title>"`.
89	
90	The per-push cadence is a project choice, not dogma — a
91	**per-close-out** model (recording a cycle's SHAs at its
92	close-out) is equally valid. The one invariant: a recorded
93	SHA must be permanent.
94	
95	## Preparation
96	
97	The cycle's first commit (`X.Y.Z-0`):
98	
99	- **Bump the version** in `Cargo.toml` to `X.Y.Z-0`.
100	- **Update `Cargo.lock`** to match — run `cargo build`
101	  (or `cargo check`) so the lockfile's `vc-x1` version
102	  tracks `Cargo.toml` in the same commit.
103	- **Move a `## Todo` item** (if the cycle has one) into
104	  `## In Progress` and the todo item should have:
105	  - A **bold title line** — that will be the chores
106	    section header, minus the `## ` prefix.
107	  - A **succinct problem statement**; add if one is needed
108	  - A **plan ladder**.
109	- **Open the [chores section](#chores-sections)** —
110	  append a `##` header with the title it records — the
111	  cycle's anticipated close-out title.
112	
113	## Work-N
114	
115	The cycle's work commits (`X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, …)
116	implement the change. As many as needed:
117	
118	- Each commit runs through the
119	  **[per-commit flow](#per-commit-flow)**.
120	- **Interim pushes** are optional (backup, progress
121	  visibility).
122	- Close-out is the only mandatory push (see
123	  [Pushing](#pushing)).
124	- **Subdivide into a sub-cycle** if a Work commit's
125	  scope grows enough (see
126	  [Sub-cycle ladders](#sub-cycle-ladders)).
127	
128	## Close-out
129	
130	The cycle's last commit (bare `X.Y.Z`) does bookkeeping
131	only — the commit body describes that bookkeeping, not
132	what happens post-squash:
133	
134	- **Move the picked-up item** from `## In Progress` to a
135	  one-line entry (with a chores `[N]` ref) in `## Done`.
136	- **Move the `## In Progress` block into the
137	  [chores section](#chores-sections)** (the title-only
138	  placeholder opened during Preparation):
139	  - Cut the problem statement + plan ladder out of
140	    `## In Progress` (drop the bold title line — the
141	    chores `##` header already carries that string).
142	  - Paste under the chores `##` header.
143	  - Sync the chores header to the **final** commit title
144	    if the cycle's scope shifted; update every anchor
145	    back-reference.
146	  - Add an `### As-built ladder` listing the cycle's
147	    commits; optional `### Outcome` notes.
148	  - Replace `## In Progress` with
149	    `_No cycle currently in progress._`.
150	- **Update `notes/README.md`** if functionality changed
151	  (new flags, new subcommands, changed behavior).
152	
153	Whether to **squash** the cycle into one commit before the
154	publishing push, or push as-is, is decided at push time —
155	see [Pushing](#pushing).
156	
157	## Numbering
158	
159	The version suffix on each commit encodes its phase —
160	the **final identifier `0` marks a Preparation**:
161	
162	- `X.Y.Z-0` — Preparation
163	- `X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, … — Work commits
164	- `X.Y.Z` — Close-out (bare version, no suffix)
165	
166	Disambiguation:
167	
168	- `-10` — Work commit #10 (final identifier `10`), not a
169	  Preparation.
170	- `-1.0` — Preparation of the `-1` sub-cycle (final
171	  identifier `0`).
172	
173	**Nesting.** Sub-cycles append another level, recursively:
174	
175	- `X.Y.Z-3.0` — Preparation of the `-3` sub-cycle
176	- `X.Y.Z-3.1`, `X.Y.Z-3.2` — its Work
177	- `X.Y.Z-3` — its Close-out
178	- `X.Y.Z-3.1.0` — Preparation of the `-3.1` sub-sub-cycle
179	
180	Sub-cycles needing no Preparation omit `.0` (`-3.1`,
181	`-3.2`, `-3`); one that grows a Preparation later adds
182	`-3.0` without renumbering siblings.
183	
184	Bump `Cargo.toml` at the start of each phase so `vc-x1 -V`
185	reports the active phase at build time.
186	
187	## Per-commit flow
188	
189	Every commit (Preparation, each Work commit, Close-out) goes
190	through:
191	
192	1. **Mark this commit `(current)`** as the first edit in
193	   `notes/todo.md > ## In Progress`.
194	2. **Do the work** (see [Iterative work](#iterative-work)
195	   for the loop-and-squash technique).
196	3. **Flip this commit `(current)` → `(done)`** in `## In
197	   Progress` — before the cargo cycle and the commit.
198	4. **Cargo cycle** (skip-able for purely-docs commits; full
199	   cycle mandatory at close-out):
200	   1. `cargo fmt`
201	   2. `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings`
202	   3. `cargo test`
203	   4. `cargo install --path . --locked`
204	   5. (re-test if anything substantive changed)
205	5. **Work review.** Stop *before* writing any description;
206	   tell the user "ready to commit." The user reviews the
207	   changes and we iterate until complete.
208	6. **Write the commit description** — see
209	   [Commit description](#commit-description).
210	7. **Commit Description review.** Show the title + body
211	   and stop. The user reviews the description. Iterate.
212	8. **Commit.** `jj commit -m "title" -m "body" -R .` for
213	   the app repo, `-R .claude` for the bot repo (`-R` last
214	   keeps the verb visible):
215	
216	   ```
217	   jj commit -m \
218	   "<type>: <short description>" \
219	   -m "<intro paragraph>
220	
221	   - file1: gist
222	   - file2: gist
223	
224	   ochid: /.claude/<chid>" \
225	   -R .
226	   ```
227	
228	**Two overrides apply:**
229	
230	- **Deviation or question** — any time the work deviates
231	  from the agreed plan, or a question arises, stop and
232	  surface it; don't push through.
233	- **ESC-ESC** — the user can interrupt at any point to pull
234	  a review or question forward.
235	
236	## Commit description
237	
238	[Conventional Commits](https://www.conventionalcommits.org/):
239	
240	```
241	<type>: <short description>
242	```
243	
244	Titles carry **no trailing `(<version>)` suffix**. The
245	version-of-record lives in `fc-config.toml`
246	(`[project].version`); it's a stable local notion (update
247	cadence TBD), useful for confirming you're running the
248	version you're testing, not a per-commit phase marker. We
249	think per-commit version-in-title is unstable on large
250	projects: many changes are in flight simultaneously, and a
251	version is only stable once merged into the main repo — so
252	titles don't carry one.
253	
254	### Title
255	
256	- ≤50 chars total.
257	- Common types: `feat`, `fix`, `refactor`, `test`,
258	  `docs`, `chore`.
259	- Favor terse phrasings.
260	
261	### Body
262	
263	[Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form) (intro + bullets),
264	wrap ≤72. Bullet content differs per repo:
265	
266	- **App-repo body**: file-by-file. One bullet per file
267	  changed (file plus a one-line gist). Sub-bullets for
268	  files with multiple distinct changes:
269	
270	  ```
271	  - path/to/file1
272	    - first distinct change
273	    - second distinct change, wrapping to next line
274	       with continuation indented 5
275	  ```
276	
277	  The file-by-file list is the source of truth for the
278	  cycle's mechanical change record. Chores carries the
279	  narrative + design, not a copy of it. Promote any
280	  "why" beyond one sentence to a chores `###` subsection.
281	
282	- **Session-repo (`.claude`) body**: bullets describe
283	  in-session activity rather than code changes.
284	
285	### Trailer
286	
287	`ochid:` as the last line of the body — see
288	[Cross-repo linking (ochid trailers)](../AGENTS.md#cross-repo-linking-ochid-trailers)
289	in AGENTS.md for the convention.
290	
291	For breaking changes, use the hyphenated `BREAKING-CHANGE:`
292	trailer key. `BREAKING CHANGE:` (with a space) is the only
293	space-separated key the Conventional Commits spec allows; the
294	hyphenated form is also valid and avoids the space ambiguity.
295	
296	## Reviewing changes
297	
298	Work review looks at the **uncommitted working-copy diff**,
299	on the way to commit. The user opens diffs in their
300	editor (Zed, VSCode); jj commands are for terminal:
301	
302	- `jj diff` — working-copy diff (uncommitted)
303	- `jj diff -r @-` — diff of the previous commit
304	- `jj diff --from <X> --to <Y>` — any two revisions
305	- `jj show -r <X>` — description + diff for one rev
306	
307	Don't `jj edit -r @-` to view a past commit — that marks
308	it mutable and shifts `@`; use `jj diff -r @-` or
309	`jj show -r @-`.
310	
311	See [Sub-cycle ladders](#sub-cycle-ladders) for the
312	close-out squash recipe and recovery; revset primitives
313	are in [`jj-tips.md`](jj-tips.md#revsets).
314	
315	## Pushing
316	
317	### Policy
318	
319	Push is **discretionary** during the cycle (backup,
320	progress visibility) and **mandatory at close-out** —
321	the cycle's result must be published.
322	
323	**Approval is per-push.** Every push — any repo, any kind:
324	cycle push, interim backup, recovery/surgery force-push —
325	happens only after the user has reviewed the changes to be
326	published and explicitly approved that specific push.
327	Approval of a plan that *includes* a push does not authorize
328	the push itself; stop and ask again at the moment of pushing.
329	
330	### Shape at close-out push
331	
332	At close-out the cycle's *work* is done; its *published
333	shape* is the remaining choice, made at push time. Surface
334	the options and get user approval before pushing — once on
335	the target, changing shape is a remote rewrite (force-push,
336	needs approval), so choose deliberately.
337	
338	- **Squash to one commit** — single entry on the target.
339	  Right for straightforward changes where the Work-N is
340	  focused on one or two files.
341	- **Merge non-ff** *(current default)* — `main` gains a
342	  merge commit (`X.Y.Z`); cycle commits stay reachable via
343	  two parents. `jj log -r ..@ -n <N>` shows the trapezoidal
344	  shape. See [Merge non-ff recipe](#merge-non-ff-recipe)
345	  for the full setup sequence.
346	- **Keep separate** — one commit per cycle entry on
347	  `main`. Use when the decomposition itself is
348	  informative. Each chores section keeps its own header /
349	  `Commits:` ref; no consolidation churn.
350	
351	Set up squash/merge before invoking `vc-x1 push`; use
352	`jj git push` directly for non-standard shapes.
353	
354	### Merge non-ff recipe
355	
356	Setting up a [Merge non-ff](#shape-at-close-out-push)
357	close-out is a fixed sequence. `<closeout>` is the cycle's
358	close-out commit, `<prev>` the previous cycle's close-out
359	(the current `main` tip), `<work-tip>` the cycle's last
360	Work commit:
361	
362	1. **Rebase the close-out into a merge** — `jj rebase -r
363	   <closeout> --onto <prev> --onto <work-tip>`.
364	   - `-r <closeout>` keeps the `<closeout>` commit in place.
365	   - `--onto <prev>` becomes its first parent (trunk).
366	   - `--onto <work-tip>` becomes the second parent.
367	
368	   Together these make `<closeout>` a merge of
369	   `<prev>` + `<work-tip>`, forming a trapezoidal commit.
370	2. **Use `jj new <merge>`** to add an empty `@` above the
371	   merge. The rebase left `@` *on* the now-content-bearing
372	   merge, which git/IDE diff views show as uncommitted;
373	   `jj new` restores the clean empty `@` on top.
374	3. **Push** — `jj git push --bookmark main -R .`.
375	
376	**Post-hoc caveat.** If the cycle was already pushed
377	[Keep separate](#shape-at-close-out-push) its commits are
378	immutable: the rebase needs `--ignore-immutable` and the
379	push force-updates `main`.
380	The standard sequence assumes the merge is set up *before*
381	the close-out push.
382	
383	### vc-x1 push wrapper
384	
385	`vc-x1 push <bookmark>` wraps per-push mechanics. See
386	`vc-x1 push --help` for current flags.
387	
388	**Current limitation**: only fully supports the
389	[Keep separate](#shape-at-close-out-push) shape; other
390	shapes need manual jj steps. Improvements tracked /
391	planned:
392	
393	- N:1 code↔bot for Merge non-ff (`## Todo` entry P1).
394	- Symmetric squash (planned, to be captured in `-2`).
395	- Per-repo bookmark names (planned).
396	
397	### .claude cadence
398	
399	**Cadence**: one push = one `.claude` commit, paired
400	with every code commit in that push.
401	
402	The `.claude` working copy accumulates session data
403	across the cycle; its change ID stays stable across
404	snapshots, `jj describe`, and the finalize commit, so
405	code-side `ochid:` trailers resolve.
406	
407	`.claude` is a linear journal — all session work lives
408	on `main`, regardless of the app-side bookmark. **Do not
409	create or maintain `.claude` bookmarks that mirror
410	app-side branches** — risks the bot steering session
411	pushes to the wrong remote ref.
412	
413	Ending a session: if the user runs `/exit` there will be
414	session information created, which we don't worry about.
415	The user can close the terminal instead and `@` will
416	remain empty.
417	
418	### Bot communication at the reviews
419	
420	Use plain prose — no insider jargon ("Gate N signal",
421	"Checkpoint N", etc.):
422	
423	- **At Work review** — summarize what changed and stop.
424	  "Work complete. Please review."
425	- **At Commit Description review** — present `$TITLE`
426	  and `$BODY` explicitly; ask permission to commit/push.
427	  Don't spell out the full `vc-x1 push ... --title ...
428	  --body ...` invocation by default.
429	- **At Post close-out review** — surface the shape
430	  options (squash / merge / keep) and the push target;
431	  wait for the user's choice before any `jj squash` /
432	  `jj rebase` / `jj git push` invocation.
433	
434	### After push or finalize: stop and wait
435	
436	After you **push** (cross the remote boundary) or launch
437	**finalize** on the `.claude` repo (`vc-x1 finalize --repo
438	.claude --squash --push <bookmark> --delay 10 --detach`) — by
439	hand or via the `vc-x1 push` wrapper — you **MUST NEVER**
440	proceed (next step, edit, tool call, text output) until the
441	user explicitly directs you to continue. **Even when the next
442	step seems obvious — wait.** Treat push-or-finalize as a hard
443	stop for the whole turn.
444	
445	`vc-x1 push` performs both as its tail stages — `push-app`
446	then the detached `vc-x1 finalize` on `.claude` — so there is
447	no gap left to speak in once the push is invoked. Put **all**
448	closing words *before* invoking it; do not purposely emit anything
449	after the tool returns.
450	
451	Why (`.claude` is the live journal finalize snapshots →
452	squashes → pushes):
453	
454	- **`--detach`**: lets the tool return immediately so the
455	  harness can keep flushing the *trailing* session-data — the
456	  tool call's own transcript and any words emitted *before*
457	  the invoke — into the snapshot.
458	- **`--delay 10`**: gives the harness time to complete that
459	  flush before the snapshot is taken. It is **not** time for
460	  the bot to do more work.
461	- **silence**: the bot must not summarize or perform any work
462	  after the tool returns.
463	
464	**Known slip**: the bot has emitted a summary after launching
465	finalize. There is no "harmless" closing line — if it's worth
466	saying, say it *before* the invoke.
467	
468	### Recovery
469	
470	- **If push exits before `finalize-claude`** (e.g.
471	  failure between `push-app` and `finalize-claude`), run
472	  finalize by hand:
473	
474	  ```
475	  vc-x1 finalize --repo .claude --squash --push <bookmark> --delay 10 --detach --log /tmp/vc-x1-finalize.log
476	  ```
477	
478	- **Run finalize again if `@` is non-empty** after the
479	  finalize's squash-and-commit (also desirable after
480	  extra activity by the bot's agents).
481	  - Why: finalize snapshots `.claude` at `--delay`, but the
482	    bot keeps writing session data while it runs — the tool's
483	    own invocation plus the bot's closing response land
484	    *after* the snapshot.
485	  - Safe to repeat: bot session data is append-only, so a
486	    re-run never conflicts or overwrites. (This could
487	    change; it is not under the user's control.)
488	  - Fix: run `vc-x1 finalize --repo .claude --squash --push
489	    <bookmark> --delay 10 --detach` another time to capture
490	    that tail; one extra pass is generally enough.
491	  - No guarantees: events outside the bot's control can leave
492	    `@` non-empty — e.g. the bot's back end may decide to
493	    squash/consolidate session data, which can take minutes
494	    and land after the snapshot. The remedy is the same: just
495	    run finalize again. This is why a single pass is never
496	    guaranteed to leave `@` empty.
497	- **Clear push's saved state** after any out-of-band
498	  recovery — `rm .vc-x1/push-state.toml` or `vc-x1 push
499	  <bookmark> --restart` — otherwise push resumes from a
500	  stale stage.
501	- **Late code-repo tweak after `push-app` succeeded**
502	  (e.g. updating AGENTS.md or memory) requires `jj
503	  squash --ignore-immutable` and a re-push; that is a
504	  remote rewrite and needs explicit approval like any
505	  push.
506	
507	## Iterative work
508	
509	When work for a single commit (the **target**) benefits
510	from incremental review, loop:
511	
512	1. `jj new -R .` — fresh empty `@` on top of the target.
513	2. Make the next round of changes.
514	3. User reviews the round (see
515	   [Reviewing changes](#reviewing-changes)).
516	4. `jj squash -R .` folds into the target and creates a
517	   new empty `@`.
518	5. If not done, go to step 2.
519	
520	Same jj mechanics as a
521	[sub-cycle ladder](#sub-cycle-ladders), but at
522	single-commit scope — the cycle's version suffix
523	doesn't change.
524	
525	## Sub-cycle ladders
526	
527	When a Work commit subdivides into a sub-cycle (see
528	[Numbering](#numbering) for suffix nesting), its Work
529	commits typically live as a local jj `@` chain and
530	**collapse into the sub-cycle's Close-out** before the
531	parent cycle continues. Ladder commits are scratch —
532	for review and bisection only.
533	
534	### Per-Work-commit contract within a ladder
535	
536	For each Work commit in the ladder:
537	
538	1. `jj new -R .` — create a fresh empty `@`.
539	2. Do the commit's work.
540	3. Run `cargo test --bins`. **Non-negotiable** — build
541	   and clippy alone miss regressions until a later
542	   commit runs the full suite, raising bisection cost.
543	4. `jj describe -m "..." -m "..." -R .` — working title
544	   only; the sub-cycle Close-out collects everything
545	   into one final commit.
546	
547	### Navigating the ladder
548	
549	Common moves:
550	
551	- `jj log -r '<base>::' -R .` — see the whole ladder
552	  from its base.
553	- `jj edit -r <prefix> -R .` — jump `@` to any ladder
554	  commit by chid prefix; useful for bisection.
555	- `jj edit @-- -R .` — quick-jump back two commits.
556	- `jj diff -r <chid> -R .` — review one commit in
557	  isolation.
558	
559	Modifications to any ladder commit rewrite it in place;
560	descendants auto-rebase.
561	
562	### Close-out: squash the ladder
563	
564	When all ladder Work commits are done and tests pass:
565	
566	```
567	jj squash --from "<base>..@-" --into @ -u -R .
568	```
569	
570	`<base>` is the parent of the first ladder commit; `-u`
571	keeps `@`'s description and discards the sources'.
572	After squash, history is linear: `<base> → @`;
573	intermediate commits are auto-abandoned.
574	
575	Then `vc-x1 push <bookmark>` as for any other commit.
576	
577	For N = 1 the squash is a no-op (`<base>..@-` is empty
578	when `@-` is `<base>`); push the single commit directly.
579	
580	### Recovery
581	
582	If a ladder commit goes wrong, back out without losing
583	prior commits:
584	
585	- **Discard the current commit.** `jj abandon @ -R .`
586	  drops it; you get a fresh empty `@` on the same
587	  parent.
588	- **Edit an earlier commit.** `jj edit -r <chid> -R .`,
589	  make corrections, then `jj edit -r <last-ladder-chid>`
590	  to return. Descendants auto-rebase.
591	- **Discard the entire ladder.** `jj op log -R .` shows
592	  the op history; `jj op restore <op-id> -R .` reverts
593	  to that point. Full undo — removes *all* ladder work
594	  after the chosen op. Use only to start over.
595	
596	# References
597	
598	- [`jj-tips.md`](jj-tips.md#revsets) — revset primitives
599	  (chid/cid, `@`/`@-`/`@+`, `..`/`::` ranges, prefix matching).
600	- [`substep-test.sh`](substep-test.sh) — script that
601	  scaffolds a 4-revision ladder under `/tmp/substep-test`
602	  for squash-recipe experiments.
603	- The per-commit `cargo test --bins` gate exists because a
604	  regression introduced in an early ladder commit can go
605	  uncaught until a later commit runs the full suite, raising
606	  bisection cost.
607

file.content:
# Cycle protocol

This protocol uses [Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). It
contains instructions on how a commit cycle is accomplished.

## Cycles

A cycle has three phases:

- **[Preparation](#preparation)** (`X.Y.Z-0`) — the cycle's
  first commit. Sets up the cycle:
  - Bump `Cargo.toml` to `X.Y.Z-0`.
  - Pick up a `## Todo` item (typically the top-ranked,
    #1) into `## In Progress` (bold title + succinct problem
    statement + plan ladder).
  - Open the [chores section](#chores-sections).
- **[Work-N](#work-n)** (`X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, …) — the
  commits that implement the change. As many as the change
  needs; each runs through the
  [per-commit flow](#per-commit-flow).
- **[Close-out](#close-out)** (bare `X.Y.Z`) — the cycle's
  last commit. Bookkeeping only:
  - Move the picked-up item to a one-line entry in
    `## Done`.
  - Move the `## In Progress` block into the
    [chores section](#chores-sections).
  - Optionally update `notes/README.md` if functionality
    changed.

A cycle's commits are published to the project remote
either incrementally or as one batch at close-out; the
result must always be published at close-out. See
[Pushing](#pushing).

**Sub-cycles.** When a Work commit's scope grows enough to
warrant its own ladder, it subdivides — `X.Y.Z-3.0`
Preparation, `X.Y.Z-3.1` / `X.Y.Z-3.2` Work, `X.Y.Z-3`
Close-out. The same three-phase shape applies recursively
at every depth. See [Numbering](#numbering) for the
suffix rule and [Sub-cycle ladders](#sub-cycle-ladders)
for the local-ladder mechanics.

## Chores sections

A **chores section** is a `##` section in
`notes/chores/chores-NN.md` recording landed work. In
general, every commit that lands on the permanent branch
should have a reference to it on a `Commits:` list in a
chores file.

The phrase **"Open" the chores section** means append a
`##` header to the current `notes/chores/chores-NN.md`
with the title it records (e.g. `## refactor: foo bar`),
followed by an **empty `Commits:`** line. The narrative
body stays empty until close-out (see [Close-out](#close-out));
the `Commits:` line is backfilled later, once the commit is
permanent (see [Commits backfill](#commits-backfill) below).

Fuller chores conventions (content rules, header sync,
design subsection pattern, `Commits:` formatting) live in
AGENTS.md [Chores conventions](../AGENTS.md#chores-conventions).

### Commits backfill

A chores section's `Commits:` line cites the commit(s) it
records, by SHA — but a SHA isn't stable until the commit
lands on a **permanent branch** (`main`, or a long-lived
release/patch branch that won't be rewritten); a rebase or
squash rewrites it on the way. So:

- A chores section is **opened with an empty `Commits:`** line.
- **Backfill once the commit is on a permanent branch**,
  where its SHA is final. A commit can't record its own SHA
  (that would change the hash), so the fill always lands one
  push later: **each push backfills the `Commits:` of the
  commits the previous push made permanent.** On a topic
  branch the sections instead wait until the branch lands —
  so no SHA is ever written that a later rebase could
  invalidate.

Use `[[N]]` refs — several as `[[N]],[[M]]` only when one
section records multiple commits (a merge non-ff close-out) —
with the commit URL + 40-hex SHA in the file's `# References`
(format in AGENTS.md
[Chores commit references](../AGENTS.md#chores-commit-references)).
A section's `##` title matches its commit title, so a rare
deliberate rewrite of a permanent-branch commit re-syncs via
`git log --grep "<title>"`.

The per-push cadence is a project choice, not dogma — a
**per-close-out** model (recording a cycle's SHAs at its
close-out) is equally valid. The one invariant: a recorded
SHA must be permanent.

## Preparation

The cycle's first commit (`X.Y.Z-0`):

- **Bump the version** in `Cargo.toml` to `X.Y.Z-0`.
- **Update `Cargo.lock`** to match — run `cargo build`
  (or `cargo check`) so the lockfile's `vc-x1` version
  tracks `Cargo.toml` in the same commit.
- **Move a `## Todo` item** (if the cycle has one) into
  `## In Progress` and the todo item should have:
  - A **bold title line** — that will be the chores
    section header, minus the `## ` prefix.
  - A **succinct problem statement**; add if one is needed
  - A **plan ladder**.
- **Open the [chores section](#chores-sections)** —
  append a `##` header with the title it records — the
  cycle's anticipated close-out title.

## Work-N

The cycle's work commits (`X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, …)
implement the change. As many as needed:

- Each commit runs through the
  **[per-commit flow](#per-commit-flow)**.
- **Interim pushes** are optional (backup, progress
  visibility).
- Close-out is the only mandatory push (see
  [Pushing](#pushing)).
- **Subdivide into a sub-cycle** if a Work commit's
  scope grows enough (see
  [Sub-cycle ladders](#sub-cycle-ladders)).

## Close-out

The cycle's last commit (bare `X.Y.Z`) does bookkeeping
only — the commit body describes that bookkeeping, not
what happens post-squash:

- **Move the picked-up item** from `## In Progress` to a
  one-line entry (with a chores `[N]` ref) in `## Done`.
- **Move the `## In Progress` block into the
  [chores section](#chores-sections)** (the title-only
  placeholder opened during Preparation):
  - Cut the problem statement + plan ladder out of
    `## In Progress` (drop the bold title line — the
    chores `##` header already carries that string).
  - Paste under the chores `##` header.
  - Sync the chores header to the **final** commit title
    if the cycle's scope shifted; update every anchor
    back-reference.
  - Add an `### As-built ladder` listing the cycle's
    commits; optional `### Outcome` notes.
  - Replace `## In Progress` with
    `_No cycle currently in progress._`.
- **Update `notes/README.md`** if functionality changed
  (new flags, new subcommands, changed behavior).

Whether to **squash** the cycle into one commit before the
publishing push, or push as-is, is decided at push time —
see [Pushing](#pushing).

## Numbering

The version suffix on each commit encodes its phase —
the **final identifier `0` marks a Preparation**:

- `X.Y.Z-0` — Preparation
- `X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, … — Work commits
- `X.Y.Z` — Close-out (bare version, no suffix)

Disambiguation:

- `-10` — Work commit #10 (final identifier `10`), not a
  Preparation.
- `-1.0` — Preparation of the `-1` sub-cycle (final
  identifier `0`).

**Nesting.** Sub-cycles append another level, recursively:

- `X.Y.Z-3.0` — Preparation of the `-3` sub-cycle
- `X.Y.Z-3.1`, `X.Y.Z-3.2` — its Work
- `X.Y.Z-3` — its Close-out
- `X.Y.Z-3.1.0` — Preparation of the `-3.1` sub-sub-cycle

Sub-cycles needing no Preparation omit `.0` (`-3.1`,
`-3.2`, `-3`); one that grows a Preparation later adds
`-3.0` without renumbering siblings.

Bump `Cargo.toml` at the start of each phase so `vc-x1 -V`
reports the active phase at build time.

## Per-commit flow

Every commit (Preparation, each Work commit, Close-out) goes
through:

1. **Mark this commit `(current)`** as the first edit in
   `notes/todo.md > ## In Progress`.
2. **Do the work** (see [Iterative work](#iterative-work)
   for the loop-and-squash technique).
3. **Flip this commit `(current)` → `(done)`** in `## In
   Progress` — before the cargo cycle and the commit.
4. **Cargo cycle** (skip-able for purely-docs commits; full
   cycle mandatory at close-out):
   1. `cargo fmt`
   2. `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings`
   3. `cargo test`
   4. `cargo install --path . --locked`
   5. (re-test if anything substantive changed)
5. **Work review.** Stop *before* writing any description;
   tell the user "ready to commit." The user reviews the
   changes and we iterate until complete.
6. **Write the commit description** — see
   [Commit description](#commit-description).
7. **Commit Description review.** Show the title + body
   and stop. The user reviews the description. Iterate.
8. **Commit.** `jj commit -m "title" -m "body" -R .` for
   the app repo, `-R .claude` for the bot repo (`-R` last
   keeps the verb visible):

   ```
   jj commit -m \
   "<type>: <short description>" \
   -m "<intro paragraph>

   - file1: gist
   - file2: gist

   ochid: /.claude/<chid>" \
   -R .
   ```

**Two overrides apply:**

- **Deviation or question** — any time the work deviates
  from the agreed plan, or a question arises, stop and
  surface it; don't push through.
- **ESC-ESC** — the user can interrupt at any point to pull
  a review or question forward.

## Commit description

[Conventional Commits](https://www.conventionalcommits.org/):

```
<type>: <short description>
```

Titles carry **no trailing `(<version>)` suffix**. The
version-of-record lives in `fc-config.toml`
(`[project].version`); it's a stable local notion (update
cadence TBD), useful for confirming you're running the
version you're testing, not a per-commit phase marker. We
think per-commit version-in-title is unstable on large
projects: many changes are in flight simultaneously, and a
version is only stable once merged into the main repo — so
titles don't carry one.

### Title

- ≤50 chars total.
- Common types: `feat`, `fix`, `refactor`, `test`,
  `docs`, `chore`.
- Favor terse phrasings.

### Body

[Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form) (intro + bullets),
wrap ≤72. Bullet content differs per repo:

- **App-repo body**: file-by-file. One bullet per file
  changed (file plus a one-line gist). Sub-bullets for
  files with multiple distinct changes:

  ```
  - path/to/file1
    - first distinct change
    - second distinct change, wrapping to next line
       with continuation indented 5
  ```

  The file-by-file list is the source of truth for the
  cycle's mechanical change record. Chores carries the
  narrative + design, not a copy of it. Promote any
  "why" beyond one sentence to a chores `###` subsection.

- **Session-repo (`.claude`) body**: bullets describe
  in-session activity rather than code changes.

### Trailer

`ochid:` as the last line of the body — see
[Cross-repo linking (ochid trailers)](../AGENTS.md#cross-repo-linking-ochid-trailers)
in AGENTS.md for the convention.

For breaking changes, use the hyphenated `BREAKING-CHANGE:`
trailer key. `BREAKING CHANGE:` (with a space) is the only
space-separated key the Conventional Commits spec allows; the
hyphenated form is also valid and avoids the space ambiguity.

## Reviewing changes

Work review looks at the **uncommitted working-copy diff**,
on the way to commit. The user opens diffs in their
editor (Zed, VSCode); jj commands are for terminal:

- `jj diff` — working-copy diff (uncommitted)
- `jj diff -r @-` — diff of the previous commit
- `jj diff --from <X> --to <Y>` — any two revisions
- `jj show -r <X>` — description + diff for one rev

Don't `jj edit -r @-` to view a past commit — that marks
it mutable and shifts `@`; use `jj diff -r @-` or
`jj show -r @-`.

See [Sub-cycle ladders](#sub-cycle-ladders) for the
close-out squash recipe and recovery; revset primitives
are in [`jj-tips.md`](jj-tips.md#revsets).

## Pushing

### Policy

Push is **discretionary** during the cycle (backup,
progress visibility) and **mandatory at close-out** —
the cycle's result must be published.

**Approval is per-push.** Every push — any repo, any kind:
cycle push, interim backup, recovery/surgery force-push —
happens only after the user has reviewed the changes to be
published and explicitly approved that specific push.
Approval of a plan that *includes* a push does not authorize
the push itself; stop and ask again at the moment of pushing.

### Shape at close-out push

At close-out the cycle's *work* is done; its *published
shape* is the remaining choice, made at push time. Surface
the options and get user approval before pushing — once on
the target, changing shape is a remote rewrite (force-push,
needs approval), so choose deliberately.

- **Squash to one commit** — single entry on the target.
  Right for straightforward changes where the Work-N is
  focused on one or two files.
- **Merge non-ff** *(current default)* — `main` gains a
  merge commit (`X.Y.Z`); cycle commits stay reachable via
  two parents. `jj log -r ..@ -n <N>` shows the trapezoidal
  shape. See [Merge non-ff recipe](#merge-non-ff-recipe)
  for the full setup sequence.
- **Keep separate** — one commit per cycle entry on
  `main`. Use when the decomposition itself is
  informative. Each chores section keeps its own header /
  `Commits:` ref; no consolidation churn.

Set up squash/merge before invoking `vc-x1 push`; use
`jj git push` directly for non-standard shapes.

### Merge non-ff recipe

Setting up a [Merge non-ff](#shape-at-close-out-push)
close-out is a fixed sequence. `<closeout>` is the cycle's
close-out commit, `<prev>` the previous cycle's close-out
(the current `main` tip), `<work-tip>` the cycle's last
Work commit:

1. **Rebase the close-out into a merge** — `jj rebase -r
   <closeout> --onto <prev> --onto <work-tip>`.
   - `-r <closeout>` keeps the `<closeout>` commit in place.
   - `--onto <prev>` becomes its first parent (trunk).
   - `--onto <work-tip>` becomes the second parent.

   Together these make `<closeout>` a merge of
   `<prev>` + `<work-tip>`, forming a trapezoidal commit.
2. **Use `jj new <merge>`** to add an empty `@` above the
   merge. The rebase left `@` *on* the now-content-bearing
   merge, which git/IDE diff views show as uncommitted;
   `jj new` restores the clean empty `@` on top.
3. **Push** — `jj git push --bookmark main -R .`.

**Post-hoc caveat.** If the cycle was already pushed
[Keep separate](#shape-at-close-out-push) its commits are
immutable: the rebase needs `--ignore-immutable` and the
push force-updates `main`.
The standard sequence assumes the merge is set up *before*
the close-out push.

### vc-x1 push wrapper

`vc-x1 push <bookmark>` wraps per-push mechanics. See
`vc-x1 push --help` for current flags.

**Current limitation**: only fully supports the
[Keep separate](#shape-at-close-out-push) shape; other
shapes need manual jj steps. Improvements tracked /
planned:

- N:1 code↔bot for Merge non-ff (`## Todo` entry P1).
- Symmetric squash (planned, to be captured in `-2`).
- Per-repo bookmark names (planned).

### .claude cadence

**Cadence**: one push = one `.claude` commit, paired
with every code commit in that push.

The `.claude` working copy accumulates session data
across the cycle; its change ID stays stable across
snapshots, `jj describe`, and the finalize commit, so
code-side `ochid:` trailers resolve.

`.claude` is a linear journal — all session work lives
on `main`, regardless of the app-side bookmark. **Do not
create or maintain `.claude` bookmarks that mirror
app-side branches** — risks the bot steering session
pushes to the wrong remote ref.

Ending a session: if the user runs `/exit` there will be
session information created, which we don't worry about.
The user can close the terminal instead and `@` will
remain empty.

### Bot communication at the reviews

Use plain prose — no insider jargon ("Gate N signal",
"Checkpoint N", etc.):

- **At Work review** — summarize what changed and stop.
  "Work complete. Please review."
- **At Commit Description review** — present `$TITLE`
  and `$BODY` explicitly; ask permission to commit/push.
  Don't spell out the full `vc-x1 push ... --title ...
  --body ...` invocation by default.
- **At Post close-out review** — surface the shape
  options (squash / merge / keep) and the push target;
  wait for the user's choice before any `jj squash` /
  `jj rebase` / `jj git push` invocation.

### After push or finalize: stop and wait

After you **push** (cross the remote boundary) or launch
**finalize** on the `.claude` repo (`vc-x1 finalize --repo
.claude --squash --push <bookmark> --delay 10 --detach`) — by
hand or via the `vc-x1 push` wrapper — you **MUST NEVER**
proceed (next step, edit, tool call, text output) until the
user explicitly directs you to continue. **Even when the next
step seems obvious — wait.** Treat push-or-finalize as a hard
stop for the whole turn.

`vc-x1 push` performs both as its tail stages — `push-app`
then the detached `vc-x1 finalize` on `.claude` — so there is
no gap left to speak in once the push is invoked. Put **all**
closing words *before* invoking it; do not purposely emit anything
after the tool returns.

Why (`.claude` is the live journal finalize snapshots →
squashes → pushes):

- **`--detach`**: lets the tool return immediately so the
  harness can keep flushing the *trailing* session-data — the
  tool call's own transcript and any words emitted *before*
  the invoke — into the snapshot.
- **`--delay 10`**: gives the harness time to complete that
  flush before the snapshot is taken. It is **not** time for
  the bot to do more work.
- **silence**: the bot must not summarize or perform any work
  after the tool returns.

**Known slip**: the bot has emitted a summary after launching
finalize. There is no "harmless" closing line — if it's worth
saying, say it *before* the invoke.

### Recovery

- **If push exits before `finalize-claude`** (e.g.
  failure between `push-app` and `finalize-claude`), run
  finalize by hand:

  ```
  vc-x1 finalize --repo .claude --squash --push <bookmark> --delay 10 --detach --log /tmp/vc-x1-finalize.log
  ```

- **Run finalize again if `@` is non-empty** after the
  finalize's squash-and-commit (also desirable after
  extra activity by the bot's agents).
  - Why: finalize snapshots `.claude` at `--delay`, but the
    bot keeps writing session data while it runs — the tool's
    own invocation plus the bot's closing response land
    *after* the snapshot.
  - Safe to repeat: bot session data is append-only, so a
    re-run never conflicts or overwrites. (This could
    change; it is not under the user's control.)
  - Fix: run `vc-x1 finalize --repo .claude --squash --push
    <bookmark> --delay 10 --detach` another time to capture
    that tail; one extra pass is generally enough.
  - No guarantees: events outside the bot's control can leave
    `@` non-empty — e.g. the bot's back end may decide to
    squash/consolidate session data, which can take minutes
    and land after the snapshot. The remedy is the same: just
    run finalize again. This is why a single pass is never
    guaranteed to leave `@` empty.
- **Clear push's saved state** after any out-of-band
  recovery — `rm .vc-x1/push-state.toml` or `vc-x1 push
  <bookmark> --restart` — otherwise push resumes from a
  stale stage.
- **Late code-repo tweak after `push-app` succeeded**
  (e.g. updating AGENTS.md or memory) requires `jj
  squash --ignore-immutable` and a re-push; that is a
  remote rewrite and needs explicit approval like any
  push.

## Iterative work

When work for a single commit (the **target**) benefits
from incremental review, loop:

1. `jj new -R .` — fresh empty `@` on top of the target.
2. Make the next round of changes.
3. User reviews the round (see
   [Reviewing changes](#reviewing-changes)).
4. `jj squash -R .` folds into the target and creates a
   new empty `@`.
5. If not done, go to step 2.

Same jj mechanics as a
[sub-cycle ladder](#sub-cycle-ladders), but at
single-commit scope — the cycle's version suffix
doesn't change.

## Sub-cycle ladders

When a Work commit subdivides into a sub-cycle (see
[Numbering](#numbering) for suffix nesting), its Work
commits typically live as a local jj `@` chain and
**collapse into the sub-cycle's Close-out** before the
parent cycle continues. Ladder commits are scratch —
for review and bisection only.

### Per-Work-commit contract within a ladder

For each Work commit in the ladder:

1. `jj new -R .` — create a fresh empty `@`.
2. Do the commit's work.
3. Run `cargo test --bins`. **Non-negotiable** — build
   and clippy alone miss regressions until a later
   commit runs the full suite, raising bisection cost.
4. `jj describe -m "..." -m "..." -R .` — working title
   only; the sub-cycle Close-out collects everything
   into one final commit.

### Navigating the ladder

Common moves:

- `jj log -r '<base>::' -R .` — see the whole ladder
  from its base.
- `jj edit -r <prefix> -R .` — jump `@` to any ladder
  commit by chid prefix; useful for bisection.
- `jj edit @-- -R .` — quick-jump back two commits.
- `jj diff -r <chid> -R .` — review one commit in
  isolation.

Modifications to any ladder commit rewrite it in place;
descendants auto-rebase.

### Close-out: squash the ladder

When all ladder Work commits are done and tests pass:

```
jj squash --from "<base>..@-" --into @ -u -R .
```

`<base>` is the parent of the first ladder commit; `-u`
keeps `@`'s description and discards the sources'.
After squash, history is linear: `<base> → @`;
intermediate commits are auto-abandoned.

Then `vc-x1 push <bookmark>` as for any other commit.

For N = 1 the squash is a no-op (`<base>..@-` is empty
when `@-` is `<base>`); push the single commit directly.

### Recovery

If a ladder commit goes wrong, back out without losing
prior commits:

- **Discard the current commit.** `jj abandon @ -R .`
  drops it; you get a fresh empty `@` on the same
  parent.
- **Edit an earlier commit.** `jj edit -r <chid> -R .`,
  make corrections, then `jj edit -r <last-ladder-chid>`
  to return. Descendants auto-rebase.
- **Discard the entire ladder.** `jj op log -R .` shows
  the op history; `jj op restore <op-id> -R .` reverts
  to that point. Full undo — removes *all* ladder work
  after the chosen op. Use only to start over.

# References

- [`jj-tips.md`](jj-tips.md#revsets) — revset primitives
  (chid/cid, `@`/`@-`/`@+`, `..`/`::` ranges, prefix matching).
- [`substep-test.sh`](substep-test.sh) — script that
  scaffolds a 4-revision ladder under `/tmp/substep-test`
  for squash-recipe experiments.
- The per-commit `cargo test --bins` gate exists because a
  regression introduced in an early ladder commit can go
  uncaught until a later commit runs the full suite, raising
  bisection cost.

53	### Issue #4 — `get_data()` returns the live `data_dict` reference, allowing torn reads across sensor samples
54	
55	`VecNavHandler.get_data()` (`src/fc.py:567`) returns
56	`self.data_dict` directly — not a copy. The read thread
57	(`read_data()`, started via `run()` on a daemon thread at
58	`src/fc.py:334`/`381`) overwrites every field of that same dict in
59	place at the sensor rate (~100 Hz, `src/fc.py:525`–`552`). The main
60	control loop binds `data = vecnav_handler.get_data()`
61	(`src/fc.py:633`) and reads several fields from it across a long
62	control block — `acceleration_y`, `pitch`, `angular_rate_y`
63	(`src/fc.py:636`,`643`,`646`). Because both threads share one
64	object with no synchronization, the reader can run between any two
65	of the consumer's field accesses, so the controller can combine
66	`pitch` from sample N with `angular_rate_y` from sample N+1 — a
67	control command built from a mix of samples that never coexisted.
68	
69	- It is a reference, not a copy: `get_data` returns
70	  `self.data_dict` verbatim, so `data` in main and `data_dict` in
71	  the reader are the same object; any write is immediately visible
72	  through `data`.
73	- No synchronization anywhere: no lock, no snapshot, no
74	  `copy()`/`dict(...)` around the producer's write block
75	  (`src/fc.py:525`–`552`) or the consumer's reads
76	  (`src/fc.py:636`–`646`).
77	- The consumer reads multiple fields spread across the control
78	  block, and the controller combines them downstream
79	  (`y_angle_error` from `pitch`, `roll_rate_y` from
80	  `angular_rate_y`, `src/fc.py:683`/`691`), so a sample boundary
81	  crossing mid-block yields an inconsistent input set.
82	- Scope of the defect — cross-field inconsistency, not corruption:
83	  in CPython a single `data["pitch"]` lookup is atomic under the
84	  GIL, so no individual value is read half-written and the dict
85	  won't crash. The failure is **tearing across samples** (fields
86	  drawn from different ticks), not a corrupted scalar.
87	- Cost: the PD controller acts on a physically inconsistent state
88	  estimate, producing erroneous servo commands. We think the
89	  window is small (the reader spends most of each tick sleeping,
90	  `src/fc.py:563`–`565`) but nonzero, so the fault is
91	  intermittent.
92	- We think the fix is to return a consistent snapshot — e.g.
93	  `get_data` returns `dict(self.data_dict)` (a shallow copy
94	  suffices since all values are immutable scalars/bools), ideally
95	  under a lock held during the reader's write block so the copy
96	  can't catch a half-updated set.
97	
98	### Issue #3 — Per-tick `os.fsync` stalls the control loop on slow SD cards
99	
100	fc's 100 Hz control loop in `src/fc.py` calls `os.fsync` on the CSV
101	file every iteration, forcing a physical SD-card commit on the
102	timing-critical thread. There is no upper bound on `fsync` latency
103	anywhere in the app -> SD card -> OS -> CPython stack, so a card's
104	internal garbage-collection / erase cycle can block the loop for
105	hundreds of ms; the loop loses its cadence and the next `print` is
106	delayed — the periodic terminal hesitation seen on Alpine (SanDisk
107	Ultra 8GB) but not rpios (SanDisk Extreme V30).
108	
109	- Reproduced and A/B-confirmed by `scripts/fsync_probe.py` [[1]]. On
110	  the Ultra card, 500 ticks @ 100 Hz: fsync p50 4.44 ms, p99 27.35
111	  ms, max 603.61 ms; tick interval max 603.74 ms. With `--no-fsync`
112	  the loop is flat at the 10 ms target (sync max 0.03 ms, 0 spikes) —
113	  pinning the cause to `os.fsync`.
114	- No latency guarantee exists at any layer:
115	  - the SD card is an opaque FTL device whose GC / wear-leveling can
116	    stall writes for hundreds of ms to seconds; the SD spec gives a
117	    busy signal plus command timeouts, but a timeout is a failure
118	    threshold, not a maximum.
119	  - ext4 `fsync` waits on the device and can amplify — an `fsync` may
120	    force a jbd2 journal commit serialized behind other writers'
121	    dirty data in the same transaction.
122	  - CPython's `os.fsync` releases the GIL, so the VecNav reader
123	    thread keeps running, but the calling control-loop thread is
124	    blocked for the card's full commit time regardless.
125	- Cost: during a stall the 100 Hz loop pauses — telemetry rows and
126	  servo commands stop for the stall duration — and the terminal
127	  output hesitates. On a fast card the stalls are small enough to be
128	  invisible, so the defect hides until fc runs on slow or worn
129	  storage.
130	- We think the only fix that bounds the loop's timing is to move CSV
131	  logging off the control thread entirely (writer thread + queue): a
132	  card stall then blocks only the writer, never the loop. Cadence-only
133	  fixes (drop fsync, sync every N rows, time-based) still run `fsync`
134	  inline, so an unlucky tick still stalls — they reduce the odds, not
135	  the worst case.

### Issue #4 — `get_data()` returns the live `data_dict` reference, allowing torn reads across sensor samples

`VecNavHandler.get_data()` (`src/fc.py:567`) returns
`self.data_dict` directly — not a copy. The read thread
(`read_data()`, started via `run()` on a daemon thread at
`src/fc.py:334`/`381`) overwrites every field of that same dict in
place at the sensor rate (~100 Hz, `src/fc.py:525`–`552`). The main
control loop binds `data = vecnav_handler.get_data()`
(`src/fc.py:633`) and reads several fields from it across a long

(`src/fc.py:636`,`643`,`646`). Because both threads share one
object with no synchronization, the reader can run between any two
of the consumer's field accesses, so the controller can combine
`pitch` from sample N with `angular_rate_y` from sample N+1 — a
control command built from a mix of samples that never coexisted.

- It is a reference, not a copy: `get_data` returns
  `self.data_dict` verbatim, so `data` in main and `data_dict` in
  the reader are the same object; any write is immediately visible
  through `data`.
- No synchronization anywhere: no lock, no snapshot, no
  `copy()`/`dict(...)` around the producer's write block
  (`src/fc.py:525`–`552`) or the consumer's reads
  (`src/fc.py:636`–`646`).
- The consumer reads multiple fields spread across the control
  block, and the controller combines them downstream
  (`y_angle_error` from `pitch`, `roll_rate_y` from
  `angular_rate_y`, `src/fc.py:683`/`691`), so a sample boundary
  crossing mid-block yields an inconsistent input set.
- Scope of the defect — cross-field inconsistency, not corruption:
  in CPython a single `data["pitch"]` lookup is atomic under the
  GIL, so no individual value is read half-written and the dict
  won't crash. The failure is **tearing across samples** (fields
  drawn from different ticks), not a corrupted scalar.
- Cost: the PD controller acts on a physically inconsistent state
  estimate, producing erroneous servo commands. We think the
  window is small (the reader spends most of each tick sleeping,
  `src/fc.py:563`–`565`) but nonzero, so the fault is
  intermittent.
- We think the fix is to return a consistent snapshot — e.g.
  `get_data` returns `dict(self.data_dict)` (a shallow copy
  suffices since all values are immutable scalars/bools), ideally
  under a lock held during the reader's write block so the copy
  can't catch a half-updated set.

### Issue #3 — Per-tick `os.fsync` stalls the control loop on slow SD cards

fc's 100 Hz control loop in `src/fc.py` calls `os.fsync` on the CSV
file every iteration, forcing a physical SD-card commit on the
timing-critical thread. There is no upper bound on `fsync` latency
anywhere in the app -> SD card -> OS -> CPython stack, so a card's
internal garbage-collection / erase cycle can block the loop for
hundreds of ms; the loop loses its cadence and the next `print` is
delayed — the periodic terminal hesitation seen on Alpine (SanDisk
Ultra 8GB) but not rpios (SanDisk Extreme V30).

- Reproduced and A/B-confirmed by `scripts/fsync_probe.py` [[1]]. On
  the Ultra card, 500 ticks @ 100 Hz: fsync p50 4.44 ms, p99 27.35
  ms, max 603.61 ms; tick interval max 603.74 ms. With `--no-fsync`
  the loop is flat at the 10 ms target (sync max 0.03 ms, 0 spikes) —
  pinning the cause to `os.fsync`.
- No latency guarantee exists at any layer:
  - the SD card is an opaque FTL device whose GC / wear-leveling can
    stall writes for hundreds of ms to seconds; the SD spec gives a
    busy signal plus command timeouts, but a timeout is a failure
    threshold, not a maximum.
  - ext4 `fsync` waits on the device and can amplify — an `fsync` may
    force a jbd2 journal commit serialized behind other writers'
    dirty data in the same transaction.
  - CPython's `os.fsync` releases the GIL, so the VecNav reader
    thread keeps running, but the calling control-loop thread is
    blocked for the card's full commit time regardless.
- Cost: during a stall the 100 Hz loop pauses — telemetry rows and
  servo commands stop for the stall duration — and the terminal
  output hesitates. On a fast card the stalls are small enough to be
  invisible, so the defect hides until fc runs on slow or worn
  storage.
- We think the only fix that bounds the loop's timing is to move CSV
  logging off the control thread entirely (writer thread + queue): a
  card stall then blocks only the writer, never the loop. Cadence-only
  fixes (drop fsync, sync every N rows, time-based) still run `fsync`
  inline, so an unlucky tick still stalls — they reduce the odds, not
  the worst case.

content.text:
Thoughts only — I think your plan is sound, and actually cleaner than rewriting the bad commit's metadata. Throwing away a trivial, never-pushed change and redoing it under the now-present rules beats surgically re-describing a commit that was made the wrong way (raw `git`, detached HEAD). Here's my read on it.

**Why it's the right call**
- The scaffolding (AGENTS.md + notes/) is what *defines* the workflow, so it should land first — then the two-line fix becomes the first piece of work actually done *under* the protocol, instead of retrofitted into a commit that predates it.
- The fix is two lines; recreating it costs nothing. There's zero value in preserving that specific commit object.

**One mechanical thing to flag:** "revert the previous change" and "reparent the scaffolding onto origin" are the *same* operation in jj. Right now the layout is:

```
@   scaffolding (uncommitted)        ← AGENTS.md, notes/, …
@-  fix commit (main*)               ← the 2-line main.rs change, local-only
◆   fa3b82f1 (main@origin)           ← "Generalize CLI…"
```

`jj abandon -r @-` drops the fix commit *and* rebases the scaffolding down onto `fa3b82f1`, which also reverts `src/main.rs` back to origin's version in the working tree. So one command does both the revert and the reparent — no separate `git revert`.

**So the full sequence I'd propose (not executing — your call):**
1. `jj abandon` the fix commit → scaffolding reparents onto `main@origin`, main.rs reverts.
2. Describe the scaffolding working copy into a real commit — `docs:`-type, ≤50-char title (e.g. `docs: add AGENTS.md + notes workflow scaffolding`), file-by-file Prose body. **No hand-written `ochid:`** — `vc-x1 push` injects it.
3. Move the `main` bookmark to that commit (it currently sits on the fix commit we're abandoning).
4. `vc-x1 push main` — cargo preflight already passes (fmt/clippy/test green); single commit = the "Keep separate" shape it supports. **Then hard stop** — I say nothing until you speak.
5. *Next turn:* redo the two-line fix as a fresh lightweight commit and `vc-x1 push main` again (its own approval + hard stop).

**Two caveats worth naming:**
- **It's two pushes, two approvals, two hard-stops** — scaffolding, then the fix. That matches "push the new code, *then* do the change," and I think separating them is correct (bootstrap vs. first real change).
- **The scaffolding push is a bootstrap, not a cycle** — it can't follow the cycle protocol (version bump / `## In Progress` / chores) because it's the commit that *introduces* that protocol. Pushing it as a plain `docs:` commit is the honest way; the cycle machinery starts applying from the fix onward.

One open question for when you're ready to act: for the scaffolding commit, do you want `docs:` or `chore:` as the type, and should I leave `Cargo.toml` at `0.1.0` (I'd say yes — no bump for the bootstrap)?

message.content: SG, but one Q first. ATM we have a stub, CLAUDE.md which points at AGENTS.md, do you still need CLAUDE.md or can we delete it?

content.text:
Keep it — I'd recommend against deleting it. Here's the reasoning:

The two files serve different consumers:
- **Claude Code (me)** auto-loads `CLAUDE.md` from the repo root. That's the guaranteed, default discovery hook.
- **AGENTS.md** is the cross-tool convention (Zed, etc.) — but Claude Code does *not* load it by default.

The `@AGENTS.md` import line in the stub is precisely the bridge: Claude Code reads `CLAUDE.md`, the import pulls `AGENTS.md` into context, so the canonical instructions reach me without being duplicated. That's exactly what the stub's own comment says it's for.

If you delete `CLAUDE.md`, the risk is that I stop auto-loading `AGENTS.md` — those instructions would silently drop out of my context unless Claude Code is specifically configured to read `AGENTS.md`. So the 4-line stub is buying you the single source of truth (AGENTS.md) *and* Claude Code coverage, for almost no cost.

Two notes:
- The cleaner-looking alternative — making `CLAUDE.md` a symlink to `AGENTS.md` — works too, but the import stub is better: it leaves room for genuinely Claude-Code-specific notes later, and avoids symlink edge cases in tooling/diffs.
- I'm reasoning from how Claude Code's file discovery generally works; these files were added mid-session so I can't use "did it auto-load" as proof this turn. If you want certainty before deciding, I can have the claude-code-guide agent confirm whether current Claude Code can be pointed at `AGENTS.md` directly — if it can, deleting the stub becomes viable.

My vote: keep the stub. Want me to verify the AGENTS.md-direct-load question first, or proceed with the abandon → push-scaffolding plan as-is?

message.content: Not symlink, I've recently learned they don't work by default in Windows

content.text:
Right — that settles it: keep `CLAUDE.md` as the import stub, no symlink. The Windows default (symlinks require Developer Mode or elevated privileges, and git won't materialize them as real links otherwise) would silently break the bridge for any Windows collaborator, while a plain file with `@AGENTS.md` is portable everywhere. Good reason to standardize on the stub.

So nothing changes in the file set — `CLAUDE.md` (stub) + `AGENTS.md` (canonical) both stay, and they go into the scaffolding commit as-is.

content.content:
vc-x1 0.65.2-1

Dual-repo commit+push+finalize in one resumable command.

Collapses today's manual Commit-Push-Finalize Flow into a
single subcommand with two interactive approval gates and a
state machine with persistent progress so interruptions can
resume without re-doing completed stages.

Stages: preflight (fmt/clippy/test) → review (approve diff)
→ message ($EDITOR / --title+--body, approve text) →
commit-app → commit-claude (skipped if clean) → bookmark-both
→ push-app → finalize-claude. Failures in commit-app /
commit-claude / bookmark-both roll both repos back via
`jj op restore` to the snapshot recorded before commit-app.
After push-app succeeds the remote boundary is crossed and
recovery is forward-only.

Non-interactive use: pass both --title and --body plus --yes
to skip the review gate. Saved state carries title/body
across resumes so only the first invocation needs them.

Usage: vc-x1 push [OPTIONS] [BOOKMARK]

Arguments:
  [BOOKMARK]
          Bookmark to advance in both repos (positional form of `--bookmark`).
          
          Accepting a positional lets the common case read as `vc-x1 push main`
          without the `--bookmark` ceremony; `--bookmark` is kept as an alias
          for scripts and for composition with other args. The two forms
          conflict if both supplied.

Options:
      --bookmark <BOOKMARK>
          Bookmark to advance in both repos (flag form; see positional)

  -V, --version
          Print the `vc-x1 X.Y.Z` banner as the first line, then continue. With
          no subcommand, prints the banner and exits.
          
          Replaces clap's auto-version (which would exit after printing): the
          banner now rides along with normal subcommand execution rather than
          gating it, so scripts can capture the version *and* the command's
          output in one invocation.

      --restart
          Clear any saved state file and start from stage 1

  -v, --verbose...
          Verbose output: -v debug, -vv trace

      --from <STAGE>
          Explicit stage to jump to (advanced / debug use)

          Possible values:
          - preflight:       Run fmt / clippy / test / install / retest
          - review:          Present diff for the first approval gate
          - message:         Compose / edit the commit message; present for
            second gate
          - commit-app:      Commit the app repo
          - commit-claude:   Commit the `.claude` session repo (skipped if
            empty)
          - bookmark-both:   Advance both bookmarks to `@-`
          - push-app:        `jj git push --bookmark <b> -R .`
          - finalize-claude: `vc-x1 finalize --repo .claude --squash --push <b>
            ...`

      --log <LOG>
          Log file path (captures all levels)

      --step
          Pause between every stage for an interactive approval gate

      --status
          Print where the saved state thinks we are and exit

      --recheck
          Re-run `preflight` even on resume (default: skip if last run
          succeeded)

      --no-finalize
          Stop before `finalize-claude` so it can be run manually

      --dry-run
          Print the exact commands for every stage without side effects

      --title <STR>
          Commit title (skip `$EDITOR` for the message stage)

      --body <STR>
          Commit body (skip `$EDITOR` for the message stage)

  -y, --yes
          Auto-approve interactive prompts (review gate, message-edit
          confirmation). Required when `--title` / `--body` aren't both supplied
          in non-interactive contexts

  -h, --help
          Print help (see a summary with '-h')

          conflict if both supplied.

Options:
      --bookmark <BOOKMARK>
          Bookmark to advance in both repos (flag form; see positional)

  -V, --version
          Print the `vc-x1 X.Y.Z` banner as the first line, then continue. With
          no subcommand, prints the banner and exits.
          
          Replaces clap's auto-version (which would exit after printing): the
          banner now rides along with normal subcommand execution rather than
          gating it, so scripts can capture the version *and* the command's
          output in one invocation.

      --restart
          Clear any saved state file and start from stage 1

  -v, --verbose...
          Verbose output: -v debug, -vv trace

      --from <STAGE>
          Explicit stage to jump to (advanced / debug use)

          Possible values:
          - preflight:       Run fmt / clippy / test / install / retest
          - review:          Present diff for the first approval gate
          - message:         Compose / edit the commit message; present for
            second gate
          - commit-app:      Commit the app repo
          - commit-claude:   Commit the `.claude` session repo (skipped if
            empty)
          - bookmark-both:   Advance both bookmarks to `@-`
          - push-app:        `jj git push --bookmark <b> -R .`
          - finalize-claude: `vc-x1 finalize --repo .claude --squash --push <b>
            ...`

      --log <LOG>
          Log file path (captures all levels)

      --step
          Pause between every stage for an interactive approval gate

      --status
          Print where the saved state thinks we are and exit

      --recheck
          Re-run `preflight` even on resume (default: skip if last run
          succeeded)

      --no-finalize
          Stop before `finalize-claude` so it can be run manually

      --dry-run
          Print the exact commands for every stage without side effects

      --title <STR>
          Commit title (skip `$EDITOR` for the message stage)

      --body <STR>
          Commit body (skip `$EDITOR` for the message stage)

  -y, --yes
          Auto-approve interactive prompts (review gate, message-edit
          confirmation). Required when `--title` / `--body` aren't both supplied
          in non-interactive contexts

  -h, --help
          Print help (see a summary with '-h')

content.text:
Agreed — and the `--help` confirms your instinct exactly. `vc-x1 push` is a full state machine that does the committing itself:

```
preflight (fmt/clippy/test) → review (approve diff) → message (--title/--body) →
commit-app → commit-claude → bookmark-both → push-app → finalize-claude
```

So a manual `jj commit` first is not just redundant, it's actively counterproductive: the wrapper's own `commit-app`/`commit-claude` stages create the commits, advance **both** bookmarks together, inject the `ochid:` trailers, and record a pre-commit snapshot it can `jj op restore` back to if anything fails before `push-app`. If I hand it an already-committed `@`, I've pre-empted the stage it wants to own and muddied that rollback point. Letting it drive keeps app + `.claude` paired and the ochid handling in one place — which is the whole reason the dual-repo wrapper exists. Good call to stop me.

So the corrected plan: **leave `@` exactly as it is** (the 10 files uncommitted) and let `vc-x1 push main` do everything.

Two practical wrinkles to settle first:

1. **The gates are interactive, and I drive Bash non-interactively.** The `review` and `message` stages prompt in the terminal. Two ways to handle it:
   - *I present `$TITLE` + `$BODY` here for your review; on your approval I run it with `--title/--body --yes`* (the `--yes` is licensed by your in-chat approval — that **is** the per-push sign-off), then hard-stop.
   - *You run `vc-x1 push main` yourself* (via `!` or your own terminal) and step through the diff/message gates interactively in `$EDITOR`.

2. **The `main` bookmark was deleted by the abandon.** `bookmark-both` *advances* `main` — `main@origin` still exists remotely (fa3b82f1), so it should re-establish cleanly, but I'd rather verify than assume. `vc-x1 push main --dry-run` prints every stage's exact commands with **no side effects** — a safe way to confirm the bookmark handling and preflight before the real run.

My suggestion: let me (a) run `--dry-run` to confirm the mechanics, then (b) present `$TITLE`/`$BODY` for your approval, then (c) the real `vc-x1 push main` and hard-stop. Want me to start with the dry-run, or would you rather run the whole thing interactively yourself?

-### Issue #4 — `get_data()` returns the live `data_dict` reference, allowing torn reads across sensor samples
-`VecNavHandler.get_data()` (`src/fc.py:567`) returns
-`self.data_dict` directly — not a copy. The read thread
-(`read_data()`, started via `run()` on a daemon thread at
-`src/fc.py:334`/`381`) overwrites every field of that same dict in
-place at the sensor rate (~100 Hz, `src/fc.py:525`–`552`). The main
-control loop binds `data = vecnav_handler.get_data()`
-(`src/fc.py:633`) and reads several fields from it across a long
-control block — `acceleration_y`, `pitch`, `angular_rate_y`
-(`src/fc.py:636`,`643`,`646`). Because both threads share one
-object with no synchronization, the reader can run between any two
-of the consumer's field accesses, so the controller can combine
-`pitch` from sample N with `angular_rate_y` from sample N+1 — a
-control command built from a mix of samples that never coexisted.
-- It is a reference, not a copy: `get_data` returns
-  `self.data_dict` verbatim, so `data` in main and `data_dict` in
-  the reader are the same object; any write is immediately visible
-  through `data`.
-- No synchronization anywhere: no lock, no snapshot, no
-  `copy()`/`dict(...)` around the producer's write block
-  (`src/fc.py:525`–`552`) or the consumer's reads
-- The consumer reads multiple fields spread across the control
-  block, and the controller combines them downstream
-  (`y_angle_error` from `pitch`, `roll_rate_y` from
-  `angular_rate_y`, `src/fc.py:683`/`691`), so a sample boundary
-  crossing mid-block yields an inconsistent input set.
-- Scope of the defect — cross-field inconsistency, not corruption:
-  in CPython a single `data["pitch"]` lookup is atomic under the
-  GIL, so no individual value is read half-written and the dict
-  won't crash. The failure is **tearing across samples** (fields
-  drawn from different ticks), not a corrupted scalar.
-- Cost: the PD controller acts on a physically inconsistent state
-  estimate, producing erroneous servo commands. We think the
-  window is small (the reader spends most of each tick sleeping,
-  `src/fc.py:563`–`565`) but nonzero, so the fault is
-  intermittent.
-- We think the fix is to return a consistent snapshot — e.g.
-  `get_data` returns `dict(self.data_dict)` (a shallow copy
-  suffices since all values are immutable scalars/bools), ideally
-  under a lock held during the reader's write block so the copy
-  can't catch a half-updated set.
-### Issue #3 — Per-tick `os.fsync` stalls the control loop on slow SD cards
-fc's 100 Hz control loop in `src/fc.py` calls `os.fsync` on the CSV
-file every iteration, forcing a physical SD-card commit on the
-timing-critical thread. There is no upper bound on `fsync` latency
-anywhere in the app -> SD card -> OS -> CPython stack, so a card's
-internal garbage-collection / erase cycle can block the loop for
-hundreds of ms; the loop loses its cadence and the next `print` is
-delayed — the periodic terminal hesitation seen on Alpine (SanDisk
-Ultra 8GB) but not rpios (SanDisk Extreme V30).
-- Reproduced and A/B-confirmed by `scripts/fsync_probe.py` [[1]]. On
-  the Ultra card, 500 ticks @ 100 Hz: fsync p50 4.44 ms, p99 27.35
-  ms, max 603.61 ms; tick interval max 603.74 ms. With `--no-fsync`
-  the loop is flat at the 10 ms target (sync max 0.03 ms, 0 spikes) —
-  pinning the cause to `os.fsync`.
-- No latency guarantee exists at any layer:
-  - the SD card is an opaque FTL device whose GC / wear-leveling can
-    stall writes for hundreds of ms to seconds; the SD spec gives a
-    busy signal plus command timeouts, but a timeout is a failure
-    threshold, not a maximum.
-  - ext4 `fsync` waits on the device and can amplify — an `fsync` may
-    force a jbd2 journal commit serialized behind other writers'
-    dirty data in the same transaction.
-  - CPython's `os.fsync` releases the GIL, so the VecNav reader
-    thread keeps running, but the calling control-loop thread is
-    blocked for the card's full commit time regardless.
-- Cost: during a stall the 100 Hz loop pauses — telemetry rows and
-  servo commands stop for the stall duration — and the terminal
-  output hesitates. On a fast card the stalls are small enough to be
-  invisible, so the defect hides until fc runs on slow or worn
-  storage.
-- We think the only fix that bounds the loop's timing is to move CSV
-  logging off the control thread entirely (writer thread + queue): a
-  card stall then blocks only the writer, never the loop. Cadence-only
-  fixes (drop fsync, sync every N rows, time-based) still run `fsync`
-  inline, so an unlucky tick still stalls — they reduce the odds, not
-  the worst case.

### Issue #4 — `get_data()` returns the live `data_dict` reference, allowing torn reads across sensor samples

`VecNavHandler.get_data()` (`src/fc.py:567`) returns
`self.data_dict` directly — not a copy. The read thread
(`read_data()`, started via `run()` on a daemon thread at
`src/fc.py:334`/`381`) overwrites every field of that same dict in
place at the sensor rate (~100 Hz, `src/fc.py:525`–`552`). The main
control loop binds `data = vecnav_handler.get_data()`
(`src/fc.py:633`) and reads several fields from it across a long
control block — `acceleration_y`, `pitch`, `angular_rate_y`
(`src/fc.py:636`,`643`,`646`). Because both threads share one
object with no synchronization, the reader can run between any two
of the consumer's field accesses, so the controller can combine
`pitch` from sample N with `angular_rate_y` from sample N+1 — a
control command built from a mix of samples that never coexisted.

- It is a reference, not a copy: `get_data` returns
  `self.data_dict` verbatim, so `data` in main and `data_dict` in
  the reader are the same object; any write is immediately visible
  through `data`.
- No synchronization anywhere: no lock, no snapshot, no
  `copy()`/`dict(...)` around the producer's write block
  (`src/fc.py:525`–`552`) or the consumer's reads
  (`src/fc.py:636`–`646`).
- The consumer reads multiple fields spread across the control
  block, and the controller combines them downstream
  (`y_angle_error` from `pitch`, `roll_rate_y` from
  `angular_rate_y`, `src/fc.py:683`/`691`), so a sample boundary
  crossing mid-block yields an inconsistent input set.
- Scope of the defect — cross-field inconsistency, not corruption:
  in CPython a single `data["pitch"]` lookup is atomic under the
  GIL, so no individual value is read half-written and the dict
  won't crash. The failure is **tearing across samples** (fields
  drawn from different ticks), not a corrupted scalar.
- Cost: the PD controller acts on a physically inconsistent state
  estimate, producing erroneous servo commands. We think the
  window is small (the reader spends most of each tick sleeping,
  `src/fc.py:563`–`565`) but nonzero, so the fault is
  intermittent.
- We think the fix is to return a consistent snapshot — e.g.
  `get_data` returns `dict(self.data_dict)` (a shallow copy
  suffices since all values are immutable scalars/bools), ideally
  under a lock held during the reader's write block so the copy
  can't catch a half-updated set.

### Issue #3 — Per-tick `os.fsync` stalls the control loop on slow SD cards

fc's 100 Hz control loop in `src/fc.py` calls `os.fsync` on the CSV
file every iteration, forcing a physical SD-card commit on the
timing-critical thread. There is no upper bound on `fsync` latency
anywhere in the app -> SD card -> OS -> CPython stack, so a card's
internal garbage-collection / erase cycle can block the loop for
hundreds of ms; the loop loses its cadence and the next `print` is
delayed — the periodic terminal hesitation seen on Alpine (SanDisk
Ultra 8GB) but not rpios (SanDisk Extreme V30).

- Reproduced and A/B-confirmed by `scripts/fsync_probe.py` [[1]]. On
  the Ultra card, 500 ticks @ 100 Hz: fsync p50 4.44 ms, p99 27.35
  ms, max 603.61 ms; tick interval max 603.74 ms. With `--no-fsync`
  the loop is flat at the 10 ms target (sync max 0.03 ms, 0 spikes) —
  pinning the cause to `os.fsync`.
- No latency guarantee exists at any layer:
  - the SD card is an opaque FTL device whose GC / wear-leveling can
    stall writes for hundreds of ms to seconds; the SD spec gives a
    busy signal plus command timeouts, but a timeout is a failure
    threshold, not a maximum.
  - ext4 `fsync` waits on the device and can amplify — an `fsync` may
    force a jbd2 journal commit serialized behind other writers'
    dirty data in the same transaction.
  - CPython's `os.fsync` releases the GIL, so the VecNav reader
    thread keeps running, but the calling control-loop thread is
    blocked for the card's full commit time regardless.
- Cost: during a stall the 100 Hz loop pauses — telemetry rows and
  servo commands stop for the stall duration — and the terminal
  output hesitates. On a fast card the stalls are small enough to be
  invisible, so the defect hides until fc runs on slow or worn
  storage.
- We think the only fix that bounds the loop's timing is to move CSV
  logging off the control thread entirely (writer thread + queue): a
  card stall then blocks only the writer, never the loop. Cadence-only
  fixes (drop fsync, sync every N rows, time-based) still run `fsync`
  inline, so an unlucky tick still stalls — they reduce the odds, not
  the worst case.

content.text:
Good instinct — `cycle-protocol.md` is generic process scaffolding meant to be reused across repos, so it shouldn't hardcode *any* one language's manifest. And your `fc_current.py` snippet actually makes the case: fc doesn't even use `pyproject.toml` — it reads a bespoke `fc-config.toml`. So you can't derive the file from the language; the concrete file is a per-project fact. That points to one clean pattern:

**Abstract in the shared process doc; bind the concrete file once, per-project.**

- **`cycle-protocol.md`** (shared, language-agnostic): refer to it abstractly — "the project's **version manifest**, named in AGENTS.md" — and never name a file. Something like:

  > The version-of-record lives in the project's **version manifest** — the file holding the canonical version string (named in [AGENTS.md](../AGENTS.md); e.g. `Cargo.toml` `[package].version` for a Rust crate, `fc-config.toml` `[project].version` for fc). It's a stable local notion…

- **`AGENTS.md`** (per-project, the canonical layer): state the concrete binding once. This repo would get a one-liner, e.g. under the Cycle Protocol section:

  > **Version-of-record:** `Cargo.toml` `[package].version`. Bump it at the start of each cycle phase.

That mirrors exactly what `fc_current.py` does — the *code* hardcodes `fc-config.toml` because that's fc's binding; the *process* only needs "there is a manifest with a version." Each repo binds its own; the shared doc stays portable. It also composes with the Python/uv section you kept — that section is just another concrete binding.

The lighter-weight alternative, if you'd rather not add indirection: keep it all in `cycle-protocol.md` but make the filename a list of examples — "the project's manifest (`Cargo.toml` `[package].version`, `pyproject.toml`/`fc-config.toml` `[project].version`, …)". Less clean (the process doc now enumerates languages), but zero cross-references.

I'd go with the first — abstract doc + per-project binding line in AGENTS.md — since it keeps the scaffolding genuinely reusable, which is what you're after.

Two questions before I touch anything:
1. Which approach — abstract+bind, or inline-examples?
2. Is `bugs.md` (already reset to the empty rdwr_vn100 template) good to keep, or did you want to hold that too?

Both choices noted. Making the two edits — abstract wording in the shared doc, concrete binding in AGENTS.md.
